text:
Mausoleum of the Julii situated in Glanum, a Roman village in s. France, modern St.Rémy-de-Provence, c. 40 BC.

Architrave inscription: 
SEX · M · L · IVLIEI · C · F · PARENTIBVS · SVEIS 
Sextius, Marcus and Lucius Julius, sons of Gaius, to their forebears.

Credit Lauren Heilman 
🏺 Ancientbluesky

Fledgling California condors at Marble Canyon in northern Arizona. 
722 (right) was photographed in December 2013, and 1150 (left) in March 2023.
Both hatched from eggs laid in the wild.

A Blue-winged Mountain Tanager enjoys some fresh fruit. These birds are common and widespread in the Andean cloud forest. I believe the plant is a Clusia sp. Birds provide an important service to the forest by spreading fruit seeds. 
🧪🌿🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

American workers have experienced record high job security during 2021, 2022, & 2023.

Only 1.0% of American employees was fired by their boss, either discharged or laid off, in a typical month in each of those years.

In a typical 2019 month, 1.2% were, a 20% higher rate.
In a 2020 month, 2.4%.
#️⃣#️⃣

the best caramelized onions you will ever make:

4-5 onions of various kinds, sliced 

5 tbsp salted butter

In a large saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the onions and mix. Turn heat to low. Cook for 4-5 hours, till onions are browned and sweet, with an almost jam-like texture.

Pasta alla gricia is an amazing old school dish in the "dry" class (ie dressed, not sauced). Very simple, it includes only the pasta, guanciale, pepper, water, and Pecorino Romano. It is rich, indulgent, and delicious.

Mom made… well, I’m loath to call it paella as its not got Chicken/Chorizo, but it has paella rice, prawns, hake, wild salmon, garlic, peppers etc & a 1/4 bottle of white wine, stock, last night’s lamb fat. We soaked the saffron.  Calling it a seafood risotto wouldn’t irk the purists. #Homecooking

Duck fat fries with garlic dip. (Dip’s a little fresh garlic, chipotle sauce, and 50/50 mayo/Greek yogurt.) 🍜🍽️

Made in the air fryer. Secret is: starchy potato cut thinner, & soaked in cold water, with a rinse. Dry them well before fat/cooking, & brush w more fat 3/4 through cooking them.

Next up: the Texas yellowstar, native to Texas; Oklahoma; Arkansas; through to Coahuila. This pretty annual herb is commonly found in prairie-like habitats across north-central and southern east Texas, specifically along the Edwards Plateau. It does best in well-draining sand and limestone. 🌿⭐️

Singleaf piñon, Pinus monophylla, is the only single-needle pine - all others have 2-5 needles in bundles. It has large seeds, piñones, that are delicious. Piñones are important to Native Americans throughout the  range of the tree. This is an old tree on the side of a canyon near Petrified Forest.

In 1960, David Latimer planted a garden inside of a bottle and sealed it shut. He opened the bottle and watered the plant in 1972 and sealed it for good. It has been a self sustaining ecosystem for 60 years.

The Texas mountain laurels are officially in full bloom! Not a true mountain laurel, it’s a small shrub-like evergreen in the pea family endemic to central and west Texas through northern Mexico. Their blooming season (March through April) fills the air with a STRONG smell resembling grape soda! 🌿

ERASERHEAD (1977)
Dir. David Lynch
United States
Avant-Garde, Horror, Cult

In an industrial town, Henry Spencer lives in an abandoned building amidst constant noise and smoke, trying to survive his angry girlfriend and the unbearable screams of his newly born mutant child.

Prairie dogs are a keystone species, meaning a lot of animal and plant species depend on them for survival. They're a food source for many different predators, their burrows provide homes for other animals, and their eating habits promote healthy plant diversity

JWST confirmed the redshift of the extremely distant galaxy GLASS-z12 to z=12.342.
This finding places it to be the 5th most distant galaxy ever discovered, existing only 360 Myr after the Big Bang.
arxiv.org/abs/2403.102...
🔭 🧪

A protostar embedded in a protoplanetary disk embedded in the dark molecular cloud LDN1527. You’re seeing an infant (100,000 years old) solar system about 447 light years away. The star itself is hidden from view by the edge-on protoplanetary disk occupying the ‘neck’ of the hourglass shaped nebula.

OSIRIS-REX was launched in Sep 2016 on an Atlas V rocket from Canaveral to a 0.78 x 1.17 AU solar orbit; it arrived at asteroid (101955) Bennu in Oct 2018, collected samples in Oct 2020, and departed Bennu for the return trip to Earth in Apr 2021. Welcome home O-REX!

When designing your game and adding a cool special input move that is on average faster than normal movement, ask yourself if you want players to just do that move the *entire game*. If the answer is no, don't make it faster than normal movement. Players will do it.

The Iron Crown of Lombardy was made in the Middle Ages. Legend maintains its central band was beaten from a nail of the True Cross. It was used in the coronation of the first Holy Roman Emperor Charlemagne on Christmas day 800 and was worn again in 1805 by Napoleon when he was crowned King of Italy.

Evidence for long-distance trade in the Irish Neolithic. These beautiful green jadeite axes from Donegal & Westmeath are c. 6000 years old. Studies have shown that the stone they are fashioned out of comes from a series of Neolithic quarries located high in the Italian alps

This rhyton, or drinking vessel, is shaped like a goat’s head & is from Greek southern Italy. Given the number of similar objects found, it’s likely a mass production piece. That said, the quality of the moulding & decoration shows production values were high 
🕰️late C4thBCE
🏛️ANU
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

2,300 years old, this astonishingly life-like bronze head of Thracian king Seuthes III ~ with alabaster & glass paste eyes (bloodshot!), copper strip eyelashes & eyebrows, & maybe evidence of old injury to cheekbone (Nat Mus Archaeology, Sofia) Photo: Krasimir Georgiev

This is the Temple of Apollo in Syracuse. It was constructed in the C6th BCE in the Doric style and is one of the first temples in Sicily with a peripteros (a colonnade of columns surrounding the inner sanctuary). Like many ruined classical temples, it’s the haunt of cats. 

📷 mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

A man is shown worshiping a donkey-headed god on a crucifix.

Text: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

About 80 % of students thought this was a swanky straw hat from the 1950s. Truth is that it’s the oldest Roman grasswork "Panama hat" extant, from Fayum in Egypt. In 2017 they radiocarbon dated it to 420-568 CE. Turns out the “Dark Ages” was really more about cool shade (Pitt Rivers Museum, UK) 🎩

This reticulated beaker has an inscription in glass letters: FAVENTIBVS. It’s an expression of good luck (perhaps short for "Faventibus ventis" – with favourable winds). The ‘cage’ of the beaker is also glass. These items are the pinnacle of Roman glass craft

🏛️📷Kunsthistoriches 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

My fav gull! 90% of them nest on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California but a small population began nesting on an island in a small lake and then on the roof of a McDonalds in Seaside, CA near Monterey. The island eroded & McD's burned down, but some friends of mine built a new island for them!

Coal-fired power generation in Germany fell almost 40% in February compared with the previous year. 14.1 GWh -> 8.6 GWh.

Warmer weather and lower demand explain a bit of that, but mainly wind power was much higher: 16.8 GWh vs 12.0 GWh.

🧪 Cat ankles!
Clouded leopards and margays are the only two types of cats that can climb headfirst down trees, owing to the unique flexibility of their ankles (tarsi). 
The tarsi of these arboreal predators rotate around ~180° so that their hind feet can grip even when they are face down/ass up.

Here is a photo taken at the start of last year : IC410 The Tadpoles Nebula. This 100 lightyear wide region of ionised gas is some 12000 light-years away from earth. The “Tadpoles” that you can see are themselves ten light-years long and are considered by NASA to be sites of ongoing star formation.🔭

Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar was born OTD in 1910. He established an upper limit on the mass of stable white dwarf stars. Above this Chandrasekhar Limit – about 1.44 solar masses – stars eventually explode and then collapse into a neutron star or black hole. 🧪 🔭

Photo: Stephen Lewellyn / AIP

PlayStation is laying off 900 people - 8% of its global workforce.

- PlayStation London Studio (EyeToy, Singstar) is shutting entirely
- Layoffs at Firesprite
- Various UK functions will be reduced
- Several studios affected
- Impact in all regions

sonyinteractive.com/en/news/blog...

If you don't understand why there are thousands of layoffs and tons of games and shows and studios are in jeopardy, one *massive* contributing factor has been an entire generation of executives who never operated in any other environment than near-zero percent interest.

The Velvet-purple Coronet is a fabulous hummingbird that looks velvety black in low light, but catch it at the right angle and their feathers shine purple! They are found in the cloud forests of Ecuador and Colombia.

Today's Gardening Advice: Need a flowering tree for a small space? This is Cercis canadensis 'Covey', also sold as 'Lavender Twist.' Flowers form all up and down the stems and the weeping form reaches about 10'. Prefers full sun to partial shade.  They don't tend to do well with dry soil/hot sun. 🌱

So gravitational waves can carry away linear momentum, meaning that when two supermassive black holes merge, the resulting object can be sharply kicked away at a blistering velocity of up to 4000 kilometres per second – theoretically, that's fast enough to fling it completely out of its host galaxy!

Three ancient Roman shoes, all discovered at the fort of Trimontium in Scotland and now on display in the Trimontium Museum, Melrose. The Romans tanned leather by soaking it in tree bark and often decorated their shoes with fancy cut or stamped decoration.

this was recorded in Bremen, February 1975, only a week after the more famous Köln concert and it's what I always wanted, Keith Jarrett playing minimalism, though minimal for him is still a lot. he finds the groove I'm talking about around 3:57 and sticks to it. but don't skip to it, let it find you

This splendid Greek bronze panoply is from the mid-C4th BCE and was made in southern Italy. The muscled or anatomical cuirass is particularly splendid and even includes nipples and navel. I can’t say it was modelled on my torso sadly. 

🏛️Rijksmeuseum van Oudeheden
📷Alexander van Loon

In addition to the oldest examples of basketry known in Europe, dating to 9500 BCE, la Cueva de los Murcielagos in Spain, is also home to the oldest known pair of shoes in Europe! (Photo Credit: MUTERMUR project). #Archaeology 🏺

Excavations underneath Basilica San Giovanni in Laterano (Rome) revealed vivid fresco in what were 2nd c. CE elite houses, followed by a complex sequence of spatial reuse and rebuilding ending up with the first ever cathedral. 
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

Using paleoproteomics (ZooMS), my team identified a tiny hominin skull fragment at Riparo Bombrini (Level M4), in the Balzi Rossi site complex in NW Italy. Its age and cultural association material of the layer (43.5k cal. years BP, Mousterian tools) mean it's very likely a Neanderthal. 🏺🧪

Hunter-gatherer societies on the Iberian Peninsula were making sophisticated baskets with decorative geometric patterns 9,500 years ago, more than 2,000 years earlier than previously thought, researchers in Spain have reported.

The shaman of Bad Dürrenberg are the remains of a 25-35 year old woman, who was buried 8600 to 9000 year ago in Germany. Around her, were the remains of an extraordinary head-dress, made from the bones and teeth of different animals such as deer, wild boar, crane and turtle.
via Dialogist//twitter

The large sardonyx Blacas Cameo, one of the Augustan ‘State Cameos’ from the imperial inner court. It depicts the ageless emperor from behind, turned to the left, wearing the aegis of Jupiter and Minerva. 

Made in Rome, ca. 14-20 CE.

Taken 21 Dec 2023 at the #BritishMuseum 🏺 

AncientBluesky  1/

Gift from Santa, the skills of the Medieval stone masons will always amaze & this perfect sun dial from 12thC Notre Dame du Lac in Le Thor, Provence, is a thing of great beauty. Younger & fitter stone mason apprentices were known for climbing to the very top of a church and carving VERY RUDE THINGS.

I just love this manuscript. European Columbines and Sweet Cherry, from Mira Calligraphiae Monumenta (a calligraphy manual!), made in Vienna by Georg Bocskay (text, 1562) and Joris Hoefnagel (illumination c. 1591-1596). 
At the Getty Museum, Ms. 20 (86.MV.527), fol. 28.

The Madrague de Giens is a Roman shipwreck off the coast of S France, sinking mid C1stBCE. It was a huge merchant ship, 40m in length. Fully laden it could hold 520 tons of cargo. The amphorae found held wine, oil & other goods like garum. Its discovery was also a breakthrough in marine archaeology

Around 2,200 years ago, the artist Hephaistion left his signature on a mosaic floor in the form of a piece of parchment held down with small drops of wax. This perfect imitation of a sheet of parchment, with one corner curled up, was discovered in one of the palaces at Pergamon.

Oldest-surviving anatomical theatre, located in Padua and built in 1594. Over the entrance is the Latin inscription: “hic est locus ubi mors gaudet succurrere vitam.” Roughly: “this is the place where death delights to help life (or the living).” 

Photo: Rolando Paolo Guerzoni.

A touch of ancient colour! Discovered in 1834 at the House of the Ancient Hunt, Pompeii (VII.4.48), this panel shows a gorgeously-attired Ariadne handing the skein of thread over to a blue-chlamys-clad Theseus. 

ClassicsBlueSky 

Image: National Archaeological Museum, Naples (9048)

The Mosaic of Bacchus, discovered in 2009 in Saint-Laurent-d'Agny near Lyon, is thought to be the oldest known representation of the god of wine on a mosaic in Gaul. It was created around AD 10-14 and decorated the triclinium floor of a Roman domus. Bacchus is crowned with ivy and holding a thyrsus.

MosaicMonday; the emblema of a large mosaic depicting Bellerophon slaying the Chimera from atop Pegasus. Dated to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE, the mosaic was found in Autun. It is now displayed in the Musée Rolin in Autun.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

This is a marvellous Roman mosaic found at Italica (birthplace of both Trajan and Hadrian IIRC). 

Its obviously very realistic in appearance, but it also seems to seethe with the energy and rage of an angry lion. Love it. 

🕰️C3rd CE
🏛️ Seville Archaeological Museum 
📷 Carole Raddato
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The cathedral of Syracuse, Sicily, is a typically wild, ornate baroque extravaganza but inside, & built into its very fabric, are the remains of the vast Doric Temple of Athena built by Gelon after defeating the Carthaginians at Himera in 480BCE. Magnificent

📷mine 🏺 AncientBlueSky 
ClassicsBlueSky

Are we doing FrescoFriday here on BlueSky? Well, regardless, enjoy these details from the gorgeous Garden of Livia - a beautiful Nymphaeum decorated in stunning technique to resemble a lush garden filled with birds. Now at the Palazzo Massimo museum in Rome!

(Pics are mine)

No, these aren’t ancient ashtrays, you decadent beasts. They’re Roman glass dishes from Corfu (left) and Egypt (right). Both date from around the reign of Augustus and they’re delightful. Anyone familiar with Venetian glasswork will see similarities with millefiori. 

🏛️BM
📷 mine

This is absolutely magnificent and unique. 

It’s an ancient British bronze helmet and is the only Celtic horned helmet ever found. It was found in the Thames by Waterloo so was likely a votive offering deposited in the river. Originally, it would have gleamed like gold

🏛️BM 📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

In the early Renaissance, it was popular for Europeans to import Islamic artisan goods, like the famous Baptistere de Saint Louis, probably originating from Mamluk Egypt but appearing in France in the late 1300s or early 1400s, signed by the artist Muhammed ibn al-Zayn

The reliquary of the jaw of St. Anthony of Padua (Basilica del Santo, Padua) is crazy enough as it is, but in the modern times they added two light sources, to illuminate it better. The lights reflect from a glass sphere that holds the jaw, looking like ghostly eyes.

(See the 3d photo for no-eyes)

Some lovely Roman glassware. This large turquoise bottle was found at the burial site of an affluent, though not elite, Romano-British couple in Verulanium (now St Albans). Although it’s a relatively simple piece, its survival is incredible & makes it special 
📷 mine 🏺 AncientBlueSky

Archaeology often evokes digging, but (re)studying collections curated in museums is also a key part of the job. This past May/June, at the Museum of Albenga, our team began reanalyzing stone tools from Arma dello Stefanin made by some of the last hunter-gatherers in NW Italy ca. 10k years ago. 🧪

Holy moly! This looks like some glorious Ottoman helmet, but no, it’s Iron Age Celtic. 

This is the Agris Helmet found in France, the work of Gallic craftsmen from c350BCE. An iron dome is coated in fine gold leaf & coral. It’s breathtaking. 

🏛️Musée d’Angoulême
📷Rosemania 🏺 AncientBlueSky
Been up to see the church at Greensted-juxta-Ongar, said to be the oldest wooden church in the world, it dates from the mid-to-late C11.

At first glance you might think that this alabaster tomb might be from Westminster Abbey but this remarkable monument to Alice Chaucer is at St. Mary, Ewelme in Oxfordshire. She was the grandaughter of Geoffrey Chaucer. Note the stone cadaver behind the tracery at the bottom.

A fascinating image of the more than human. A terracotta antefix showing a woman, possibly Artemis or an Amazon, on horseback with a goose(?) picking food beneath the horse’s hooves. 

Etrusco-Campanian, about 525-500 BCE, From Capua. Held by the British Museum AncientBlueSky 🏺

A Roman terracotta oil-lamp, decorated with a scene of a chariot race in progress, with a driver in charge of a 4-horse quadriga team. It was made in Italy just under 2000 years ago, & it may have been a mass-produced piece produced for fans of the races.

In AD 130, probably on the night of 24 October, during the sacred annual festival of the Mysteries of Osiris, the beautiful Bithynian youth, #Antinous, drowned in the Nile and became the last god of the ancient world, worshipped for centuries, throughout the Empire. #Egypt #Rome

Late Bronze Age baby-feeding vessels from Vösendorf, Austria, about 3000 years old. They have a tiny hole in the tail, so you can fill them with milk, and then an infant would hold it, and suck the milk through the tail. Ingenious and so pretty! Photo: Enver-Hirsch / Wien Museum

Don’t know about you but I love funerary reliefs from Palmyra. I’ve seen many in many museums & they’re so distinct. When you see one, you know at once it’s Palmyrene. They’re a wonderful example of cross-cultural exchange with clear Roman, Greek & Asian influences 

🏛️BM
📷mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Not all ancient stone tools are for cutting and hitting stuff. Some are just shiny pebbles that look nice.

More than 20,000 years ago, in what is now France, people collected and carried small stones that may have had magical powers, or been used in games🏺🧪

journals.plos.org/plosone/arti...

I’m a big fan of our leporine pals, but putting aside this bias for now, this Greek-Sicilian coin is a masterpiece of silver relief work. Just look at the patterning in his ears, the individual whiskers, the taut body. Extraordinary. 

🏛️Mandralisca Museum, Cefalu
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

#ClassicsTober23 17: Atalanta

A powerful figure from Greek myth here depicted by Tyler Miles Lockett slaying two centaurs after they attempted to rape her. Atalanta was a devotee of Artemis and like the huntress herself sought a life of independence.

🏺ClassicsBluesky

Love the old and new writing formats in this 4thC mosaic of Jesus and apostles. Jesus holds a #scroll while Paul holds a #codex - perhaps representing the Old and New Testaments? Anyway, it’s an early representation of the codex in Roman Christianity. Basilica of Sant’Lorenzo, Milan #bookhistory

Mosaic floor depicting a dog and a knocked-over gold vessel. Discovered in 1993 during construction of the new Alexandria Library, Egypt. Now currently in the Greco-Roman Museum in Alexandria.  Width: approx. 70 cm. Date: approx. 200-100 BC.

The Church of Saints Theodore was the first katholikon of Brontochion Monastery, an important monastic institution in Late Byzantine Mystras. It was built by its abbot Pachomios between 1290 and 1295.

It’s Trier (or Augusta Treverorum) in western Germany. Here the Porta Nigra (started 170), the Aula Palatina (early 4th century), the imperial baths (also early 4th century) & the amphitheatre (mid-2nd century). Incredible remains.

📷 all open source
🏺 AncientBlueSky

4th century AD fresco of Diana; Via Livenza hypogeum in Rome.  
Often used to indicate a subterranean temple or tomb, the word hypogeum stems from two Greek words: hypo (under) and gê (earth), and can refer to any building built underground.

🏺 Ancientbluesky

The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian, known for the Colosseum, is also more humbly associated with public toilets in Italy and France, known as Vespasiano and Vespasienne. This association arose as a result of a tax he imposed upon the collection of urine. 

ancientbluesky 🏺

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

4 bald eagles (1 adult & 3 juvies, perhaps the triplets born last year on the lake) trying to hunt a cormorant or kleptoparasitize it, aka harassing it until it gives up fish that it caught - a strategy often used by eagles since they are big enough to bully other animals to steal food! #SciComm 🧪🦑🦈

New bowerbird paper! Male great bowerbirds build display courts that are linked to mating success, and we found that object availability 🧱 rather than male building ability 👷 explains variation in court quality rb.gy/vznmm7

“The left side of its body was covered in shimmering spring-green feathers, the classic coloring for females. Its right side, however, was iridescent blue, the telltale marker of a male. The bird appeared to be a bilateral gynandromorph: female on one side and male on the other..."

(gift link)

Ridgway’s rail! 🪶

This endangered species is easily heard in parts of the Bay Area, but very hard to see as they skulk in marsh grass. At Arrowhead Marsh, I’ve seen one just twice in 15 trips—fleetingly, at a distance. 

But yesterday, because of the very high tide, long, clear sightings of FOUR.
My favourite photography subject at the moment is the kuaka (bar-tailed godwit). It’s about this time of year that they set off on the longest migration in the world - from Aotearoa to Alaska. 🪶

Endangered California gnatcatcher in the wonderful coastal sage habitat here down by the Mexican border in the Tijuana River Valley open space preserve. In the first shot he is doing his mewing call that sounds like a kitten. 🪶

Female Red-winged Blackbirds are acrobatic insect hunters! 

A few years ago I happened upon one dancing on pond muck - I was entranced. Now I go look for them and feel joy at seeing and hearing their little contact calls to fledglings on the shore. 🪶🌿
A white-tailed ptarmigan along a trail up La Plata Peak, CO. The smallest grouse, it stays at in the alpine/subalpine year round, mainly eating willows. It's been recorded nesting at over 13,000 ft 🪶

Two nēnēs at WWT Slimbridge! Native to Hawai'i, and the rarest goose in the world, at one point numbering only 30 birds due to hunting and introduced predators like mongoose, pigs, and cats. Decades of conservation efforts have helped reintroduce the nēnē and native numbers are over 3,800 today 😊🪶
Northern Fulmars aren't normally a beach birds but windrows of copepods were making for an easy meal so large rafts of fulmars were following the banquet. Last June along Jan Mayen, north Atlantic 🪶🦑

3rd plumage white-tailed eagle.

The white-tailed eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla), known for its distinctive white tail in adulthood, is one of the largest birds of prey in Eurasia, thriving near large bodies of water.

Photo taken in Lohja, Finland, February 2024.

A Torrent Tyrannulet (Serpophaga cinerea) delivering some snacks to the kids. These tiny flycatchers (about 10 cm or 4 in) live on fast moving rivers and streams and catch insects right at the edge of the water. I found this nesting pair in March of this year. #birds 🪶#costarica

A park ranger in Santee SP in SC once pointed out to me that the round nesting holes in the trees in the middle of Lake Marion are from Pileated Woodpeckers, but the large oval ones were made decades ago by Ivory Billed Woodpeckers, and this fact honestly haunts me - all that's left is a void. 🪶

I've seen 72 species in Texas. 68 of them were before breakfast today at Audubon's Mitchell Lake preserve. What a place! Black-bellied whistling duck, Couch's kingbird, Bewick's wren, loggerhead shrike, ladderback woodpecker, white-faced ibis & vesper sparrow were all lifers.🪶

kirtland’s warblers are the rarest breeding warbler in the united states. they’re also tail-bobbing chonks with no fear of humans and a peculiar love of fire. this one has a pink mandible which means it was born just a few weeks ago!

Swans in Europe, Asia, and the Americas are white. For centuries in Europe, there were idioms like "might as well look for a black swan" which was assumed to be impossible.

But all along, Australia was full of black swans 🪶 They're pretty easy to find here.
Minnippi Park, Brisbane
#ausbirds

This spring, when doing partial garden tidying, I left the dead swamp milkweed stems tall because birds liked to perch there. I'm so glad I did; I discovered that female Baltimore orioles carefully work the fibers out of them, creating beautiful long string for weaving their nests. Not only that, +

A Little Blue Heron and some White Ibises doing some afternoon fishing.

Ibis Fact: White Ibis nests can have nests from ground to as high up as 50ft 

L.B. Heron Fact: These Herons have white feathers that transition to its blue color over the first year of their lives/

#FloridaWildlife #Birds

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

Since I’ve been having some lovely conversations about American folk horror, today’s book recommendation is Maynard’s House by Herman Raucher (1980).  A Vietnam vet with PTSD “inherits” a house from a fallen soldier in a remote area of Maine and decides to hole up there for the winter

FIREBUGS by Nino Bulling is a fantastic existential-crisis-right-beneath-the-surface book; Bulling's cartooning is at the same time sparse and explosive, perfectly fitting a story whose silences feel like drowning more than anything else. Glad D&Q is giving this book a wider release.

Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. Her haunting work grapples with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the legacy of the Holocaust, drawn in a style that feels like a fresh amalgam of Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz.

What is after capitalism

The paucity of our speculative fiction betrays that whatever it is, it is a future that capitalism itself cannot imagine. It is helping us to know that it is a system for children that we have grown past. Everything must die. It is preparing itself.

A hailstone more than twice the size of a softball fell on Italy this summer. (The world record was a bowling-ball-size one in South Dakota) Here's how and where climate change might be making hailstorms more likely 🧪

Half the world's lakes are located at high latitudes, and new research in the journal Nature Water has found that they are warming at a rapid pace.
phys.org/news/2023-11...

Looking back over the past 2000 years, global temperatures slightly cooled until the 1800s.

The subsequent rapid warming is mainly due to increasing CO₂ in the atmosphere because of burning fossil fuels. 

The physics has been understood for decades.

We are causing the warming.

There's an energy revolution happening right now.

The price of Solar Energy has gone down by a factor of 100 between 1977 and 2013, and by a factor of 4 in the last 10 years.

Solar Energy and wind energy is now the cheapest energy to install – cheaper than fossil or nuclear.

In public discourse on climate change, it is common to look for a root cause. Some say it’s corporate greed, some say consumer behaviour, some say lacking political will. Seeking a root cause is a symptom of linear thinking. In complex systems, there are no root causes, only causal loops.

Increased atmospheric CO₂ is expected to expand the Hadley cell, leading to more droughts in the subtropics and rainfall in the tropics.

A study finds that CO₂ removal (CDR) will not return the Hadley cell to its original state for centuries.

It’s best not to emit CO₂.

If humanity is to live well and justly while keeping global warming below 1.5°C, then by 2030, according to Oxfam, the wealthiest 10 % of people will have to reduce their consumption-related emissions to just one-tenth of 2015 levels - giving the poorest 50 % space to meet basic consumption needs.

Some perspective on the North Atlantic sea surface temperature:
- it has been setting daily record highs every day since March 6
- it has been warmer than any time during any previous year on record since July 25
Severe marine heatwaves have been scattered all around the basin.

In the U.S. from 1978 to 2021, the typical worker’s #wages grew just 18%. The wages of the top 1% of earners, however, grew 385%. #CEO pay grew by more than 1,000%.

unusualwhales.com/news/billion...

minimum wage increases raise the employment rates of older workers

the labor supply of low-wage, retirement-age workers is particularly sensitive to labor market conditions: a 10% increase in minimum wages reduces retirement benefit claiming by the same amount as a 1pp drop in the unemployment rate

Pay inequality in the UK is falling: 
- minimum wage is cutting hourly low pay fast (lower earners move towards the middle) 
- weekly low pay is at last also falling (low earners have stopped reducing the hours they work) 
- the middle's generally seeing faster pay growth than top
The Fed's preferred Core PCE price index has been 2.0% annualized for two straight quarters, a return to the Fed's target with almost clinical accuracy after two years of high inflation.

Based on the latest data, we now know more jobs were created in 2023 than in any year of Trump's presidency.
 
What's more, the unemployment rate has been below 4% for 23 consecutive months — a streak unseen since the 1960s.

1984 is often cited as a banner year for the US economy -- giving Ronald Reagan a landslide re-election. 

In 1984, unemployment was 7%, inflation was 3.8%, and GDP growth was 6.9%. 

Today, unemployment is 3.9%, inflation is 3.2%, and GDP last quarter grew at a rate of 5.2%.

Productivity now appears to be growing at 2.2% year over year for nonfarm output and 2.4% year-over-year for all output.

We rarely see this type of productivity growth outside of recession-induced distortions (when businesses shed labor faster than they shed output)

Evidence for the "bad vibes" economy is simple:

Hard to overstate how impressive the job market is:

Things you do NOT need to wait for to get started with exercise:

- getting your diet "sorted" 
- having a perfect plan
- living closer to a gym
- having a gym membership at all
- knowing how to do the exercises perfectly (gotta do em badly first to get better) 
- the right mood

I’ve been trying to eat a bit healthier and exercise this more to improve my (frankly abysmal) heart health. While not a primary goal, I’ve lost a bunch of weight as a side effect. This morning, with 4 days left in the year, I crossed the threshold of losing 100 lbs since 1/1

Little tip that helped me with eating disorder recovery: change the units on your scales to ones you don't understand.

I grew up knowing pounds & stone, so changed it to kg. Now all I see is numbers - what I weighed then, what I weigh now - no value judgments attached.

Weight lifting has really changed the way I think about food/nutrition for the better. Before, my idea of healthy eating was all about reduction--eating as little as possible without collapsing, really. Now I think of it in terms of nourishing myself, giving myself strength. It rules.

One of the best and laziest things you can do for your shoulder and upper back health is resistance band pull aparts. You can do a ton of them, all day long, toss em into any empty spaces in workouts, etc. Impossible to do too many, can do em anywhere anytime.

Make it a stim, and you're golden.

I've written a lot on Twitter about people beginning strength training, and all of it can be condensed to:

Pick any reputable program you like the look of that actually fits into your life, and do what it says for 6 weeks. 

Then advice will be useful. Before then it won't be.

Higher ed is the most poorly reported on sector of society. Journalists think that because they went to college, they understand how universities function. Hence, & in contrast to how other industries are covered, journalists don’t defer to those who work in universities to shape reporting.

Here’s my non-helpful take: when socioeconomic inequality is high, and higher education stratification is steep (specially when institutional position is determined partly by undergraduate admissions), there is no way to technique your way out of privileged students’ domination of privileged places.

How bad is the lack of socio-economic diversity in economics? Read our Q&A with Stone Center Affiliated Scholar Anna Stansbury of MIT Sloan about how economics compares with 14 major disciplines on 1st-generation college grads and how this intersects with race.

The most important announcement came when physicist Roy Kerr presented a solution of the field equations of general relativity describing a rotating black hole. It describes the exterior of essentially every black hole in the Universe.

Jupiter’s Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system. Its surface is covered in ice and it’s believed to have a subsurface salt water ocean. Along with that, Ganymede is the only known moon with an internally generated magnetic field, making the Galilean moon very alluring.

This is the North America nebula region in the constellation Cygnus and covers the area of 4x the size of the full moon in the sky. This patch of ionized hydrogen lies about 1800 ly from Earth. I imaged this with an OSC camera, a WO RedCat 51 and 2 Antlia narrowband filters from my deck in August.🔭

Firefly Aerospace has confirmed the launch at 0228 UTC Sep 15 of VICTUS NOX, and Space Force has cataloged object 57861 in a 476 x 531 km x 97.3 deg, 1804 LTDN sun-synch orbit, confirming successful launch. Object's ground track consistent with launch from Vandenberg at the specified time

Happy Rotating Black Hole Day!

Roy Kerr submitted his paper describing the spacetime around a rotating black hole OTD in 1963.

Physicists had been searching for decades for this solution to Einstein's field equations. It describes, at least approximately, every black hole in the Universe. 🧪 🔭

The OSIRIS-REX main spacecraft, now renamed OSIRIS-APEX, is passing through the perigee of its hyperbola and heading  back out towards solar orbit to visit asteroid Apophis, while the sample return capsule (SRC) is now in the atmosphere over Utah

composite image of saturn in the infrared from cassini. the icy rings are blue, thermal radiation from below appears red, backlighting cloud layers, and the aurora of the southern hemisphere is bright green, where particles drawn by the magnetic field precipitate through the atmosphere. 🔭

The most energetic single particle ever detected, a cosmic ray dubbed the "Oh-My-God" particle, was observed by the Fly's Eye Cosmic Ray Detector OTD in 1991.

Its energy was about 3.2 x 10²⁰ eV ~ 51 J, equivalent to a baseball moving at almost 60 mph. 🧪 🔭

Image: Olena Shmahalo/Quanta Magazine

NASA is sending a software patch to the Voyager 1/2 probes
 
Patch is sent via 16bps signal to computers built in 1970 running for 46 years in interplanetary space

The patch will travel 18hrs on a signal akin to seeing candle light from 10,000km away

We have lost the skill to build stuff well...

Another personal highlight for the 20th anniversary of the launch of Spitzer. Infrared images taken in 2004 and 2016 of deeply embedded, young (Class 0) protostars show three undergoing luminous eruptions as they gulp down gas. All three discovered by #WomenInStem: see Safron+2016 and Zakri+2022. 🔭

The gravitational waves measured by IPTA have wavelengths ~10 light-years.  TEN LIGHT-YEARS! It is literally a galactic scale observatory! Locally (on Earth scales), these GWs don't look like waves, so LIGO can't detect them. You need a galactic observatory for these. WOW.

Cred: David J Chapman

The OSIRIS-REX asteroid sampler, returning from Bennu, entered the Earth's gravitational sphere
at 2200 UTC Sep 21 on a 22 x -33339 km x 43.3 deg hyperbolic orbit.
(The negative apogee value is a formal number describing the shape of the hyperbola, corresponding to e =1.62)

OSIRIS-REX ejected its capsule at 1042 UTC and made a divert burn to raise its perigee to 774 km (compared to the capsule perigee of 22 km). The two objects are now descending through geosync altitude (36000 km) at about 22 S latitude

An image taken with a 8.2" telescope. It's a 17m exposure, 17 arcmin on a side, limiting mag V ~20, 4 arcsec pixels. It doesn't look like much. But the telescope is on New Horizons out in the Kuiper belt. It has the darkest sky ever seen. Great for the cosmic optical background of the Universe.

Dec 17, 1972. Apollo 17 astronaut Ron Evans performs a trans-Earth spacewalk to retrieve film canisters.

He's traveling at around 36,000 km/hr (22,000 mph).

1 km below the Earth's surface is what looks like an early prototype for a Star Trek holodeck, but it's actually a monstrously huge trap built for the tiniest of objects known to exist. 

This is the Super-Kamioka Neutrino Detection Experiment, or SuperK.

🔭 Here's the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mt Wilson Observatory in California. 100" was world's largest from 1917 until the 200" was finished at Palomar. in 1948. It was here that the galaxies were proven to be apart from the Milky Way & expansion of the universe was discovered. #TelescopeTuesday

The Extremely Large Telescope that a European organisation is building in Chili is ready in 2028 and will provide images 16 times sharper than those of Hubble. This telescope is able to take direct images of earth-like planets that orbit other stars.

ESO-website about this project: elt.eso.org

Interesting article about the magnetosphere. The lack of a magnetosphere is one of the main hurdles of terraforming another planet like Mars since without that a planet's atmosphere blows away in the solar wind. Mars had a magnetosphere similar to Earth's once but it disappeared when the core cooled

This is really weird: Despite being 4.5 *billion* km from the Sun, Neptune's clouds wax and wane with the solar magnetic cycle! Even better, it's not clear why.

You’ve seen our moon in the sky with ‘Earthshine’, light reflected off the Earth that lets you see the dim ‘dark’ side. Well, this happens in other places in the solar system: Here’s Saturn’s moon Mimas with Saturnshine!

Man City winning the Club World Cup is such a fairytale. Amazing what can be achieved with £2 billion, a manager with an offshore account, a suspended 2-year ban from European competition and 115 charges of financial irregularity!

soccer is the anime of sports (foreign in sometimes inscrutable ways, popular everywhere but misunderstood, fans are insane) 

baseball is more like tabletop RPGs (grounded in tradition, obsessed with data and storytelling, abounding in irreverent humor)

I have worked in the games industry for almost 20 years. My first layoff hit hard. The place was my home and the people were my friends. Also, I had landed my dream industry, would I really be lucky twice?

I learned a few things.

Don't make your job your home. Enjoy the hell out of it.
Game Workers of Southern California say the industry has laid off 8,000 workers in 2023 despite record projected profits of $180 billion.
The game Unpacking is fanciful wish-fulfilment about having lots of nice things, being financially independent, owning your own home... It's an absurd power fantasy. It's basically Doom

Hades won a ton of awards, and the lesson gaming execs took from that wasn’t “allow your creative weirdoes the freedom to try new ideas” or “take the time to polish things instead of rushing them out the door” or even “a team that feels secure will make more money.” 
It was “more roguelikes!”

Gamedev needs unions. 

Not just for QA, localization, and community. For all of us, whether you code, make art, produce, animate or build servers. Only unions can truly change the industry’s bad treatment of its workers, from crunch and Bro culture, to unceremonious layoffs during record profits.

I need to put this into its own article at some point because I get tired of repeating that mechanisms are not genres

worker placement is just drafting, there is no true definition of "worker placement game"

even video games aren't as susceptible to this, few people say "I like double jump games"

2023 was one of the best years in games in terms of the sheer amount of quality titles released. It was also one of the worst years of games due to the insane amount of layoffs in spite of "record profits" being touted across the board. Kind of feels like a brilliant star just exploded supernova.

the (more or less) failure of VR is evidence that games aren’t really about “immersion” per se. what you want are small details, novelties, made by people. things that say “yeah i thought of that too”

not immersion, but a conversation with developer, as long and deep as you like.

Think a lot of bad discourse comes from things like “D&D is a bad game” being read (and, let’s be honest, at least sometimes intended) as “if you have fun playing D&D you are Gaming Wrong” but the former is pretty true and the latter is solidly false and this isn’t a contradiction

The wildest thing about game development is that doing one thing that makes a player go "ah, that's cool" for 3 seconds is a months long multidisciplinary effort. Whenever you play a game, you are like a dauphin surrounded by an army of tiny elves working ceaselessly at trying to make you happy.

Asmodee’s owner Embracer Group has confirmed it has begun “restructuring” at the tabletop gaming giant, as part of a massive cost-cutting programme which has already seen Embracer shed more than 900 jobs: 

boardgamewire.com/index.php/20...

Millions of kids design games on Roblox.

But the company pays in Robux, and doesn't let you cash out until you hit 30,000. That should be $300, but after the company cut it's just $105.

So kids are being underpaid, while the CEO took $232 million last year.

I'm so incredibly tired of the "NO ONE WILL PLAY ANYTHING BUT D&D" whine I hear. 

There is no mystical solution to D&D being super popular. My 5,000 copy game is never going to approach D&D popularity, yet LOTS of people play it. 

The only solution? Write and run better games.

ok here is the thing about making a video game.

a good idea needs every person on the team to align around it and make it happen.

a bad idea only needs one person.

and you have to do this every day.

With the massive game industry layoffs, scammers are out in force to rip off desperate game devs. If you're approached about a job opportunity:
*Verify the position on the official studio site, if possible
*Don't share info like your SSN or banking numbers
*DON'T WORK FOR FREE!

My best estimates indicate about 9000 layoffs in the games industry in 2023. We haven't finished February 2024 yet, and we're already at around 8000 for the calendar year. This is just based on the ones we know about.

Microsoft said Thursday that it would eliminate 1,900 roles in its video game division, including at Xbox and Activision Blizzard, the maker of games like Call of Duty and Guitar Hero. The nearly 9% reduction comes three months after the tech giant completed its $69 billion acquisition of Blizzard.

Cassin’s Finch brings #BirdGlamour to conifer & quaking aspen forests in western North America! Similar to the House Finch but with a pointier crown, Cassin’s Finches are excellent mimics and add other species’ songs to theirs. They’re also a bit salty: they love salt and visit mineral deposits. 🐡🧪🐦

Ingenious descent profile of the first soft-landing on the surface of the Moon, Luna-9, in 1966, where the lander was encased in a pressurized 'balloon,' which bounced several times on the Moon before splitting into 2 halves and then releasing the spacecraft which then deployed all its instruments.

For MosaicMonday, from the Archaeological Museum of Sparta, a mosaic fragment depicting a rather shifty looking Alcibiades. The 5th century BCE statesman is identified by the mosaicked inscription. Found locally in Sparta and dated to the 1st to 4th century CE.

Feeling volcanic today, so here's some #Fieldwork prettiness for you! 

These basalt columns are found at Devils Postpile near Mammoth Lakes, California. After ponded lava filled the San Joaquin River valley 80,000 years ago, it cooled toward the flow's center, shrank, and cracked into polygons. ⚒️🧪🌋

The 'Venus of Willendorf' was carved from limestone & tinted with red ochre by an unknown artist around 25,000 years ago.

Found at a Paleolithic dig site in Austria in 1908, it resembles other "Venus" figures from same period: it emphasizes aspects of female fertility.

But why?

Picking up the Irish Archaeology Alphabet with County Louth 🏺 

Ireland's smallest county packs a big punch when it comes to heritage. It has the finest high cross in the country at Monasterboice, as well as one of the best castles at Castleroche. The Proleek Dolmen isn't bad either!

Currently on display at the Cluny Museum in Paris, this ancient spear head made from rock crystal was found in south west France and probably dates from the Ice Age. Although its shape is not unusual, the use of the crystal suggests it had special significance.

We were happy to find a pair of Powerful Woodpeckers in Ecuador, where they aren't very common. If you wonder what an Ivory-billed Woodpecker looked like in the wild, the Powerful Woodpecker is in the same genus and looks similar.
Female (L), Male (R) 
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

American flamingos are one of the largest species of flamingo. Found in the Caribbean and along the northern coast of South America, these extraordinarily social birds live in flocks that sometimes number in the thousands. A pigment in their food gives their feathers their flamboyant pink color.

Here’s some travel inspo for Bird of the Week: The Blackpoll Warbler has the longest migration of any North American warbler. Each fall, most Blackpolls migrate over 2,000 miles across without stopping, sometimes flying over 80 hours straight! Learn more at 
bit.ly/3ReK8Lb. 🪶

Two things people do wrong with French toast:

One, use thin bread. Yuck. Should be 3/4” thick, or 1”. 

Two, don’t soak it long enough. I’ll be giving my bread an hour to soak up all the egg. If you soak too short, the inside of the bread gets no egg or flavour. 🍜🍽️

Cardinal Basil! A highly fragrant basil that grows to 24” tall. Bright green leaves contrast large, wine red flower clusters that fade to a dusty plum. Beneficial to pollinators, aroma deters common garden pests. 🌱💚

The plant with the blue-green leaves is, surprisingly to me, actually a fern! Coffee fern/coffee cliffbrake. It grows on rocky outcrops in the chaparral and other drier environments, and its leaves change from green to purplish red as they age (fern in the background is a polypody of some sort) 🌿

Crocus speciosus
Aka: Bieberstein's crocus
Native to northern and central Turkey, the Caucasus, northern Iran, Crimea and Bulgaria
A bulbous perennial that flowers without leaves in autumn, leaves are produced after flowering in the spring
Multiplies quickly

I planted this Fuschia in the ground last summer because it got aphids and I just wanted it to live or die. It inexplicably overwintered (zone 6), regrew, and bloomed beautifully. Now that I know it can, I'm going to mulch it and cover it with snow and see if we can make it through another winter! 🌱

M42, the Orion Nebula, is the nearest stellar nursery to Earth. New stars are formed here in the hot gasses of the nebula, and even some small star systems are being born! At 1500 light years away, it is far away, but close enough to appear as a smudge in the sky to the naked eye.

On Oct 5, 1923, Edwin Hubble discovered a variable star in Andromeda. This allowed him to calculate the distance and discover that these fuzzy patches were not clouds in our own galaxy, but entire galaxies themselves. 

The universe got a lot bigger that day.

In honor, I took this last night.

Wood is rarely preserved in archaeological contexts. A Late Bronze Age grave from Behringersdorf in southern Germany is an exception. It has parts of a wooden scabbard and of a quiver with bronze arrows. NHM Nürnberg #archaeology 🏺

The Löwenmensch figurine, also called the Lion-man of Hohlenstein-Stadel, is a prehistoric ivory sculpture discovered in Hohlenstein-Stadel, a German cave, in 1939. Determined by carbon dating to be 35,000-41,000 years old, it is one of (if not *the*) oldest anthropomorphic art pieces.

Basalt carved statue of a standing lion from the Sumerian city of Eridu, dated to the mid 3rd millennium BCE. Eridu is thought to be the earliest city built by the Sumerians, founded c. 5400 BCE on the southern marshes of the Euphrates River, Iraq. #AncientSiteSunday AncientBlueSky 🏺

A childlike and rather terrifying cupid pipes to Heracles in the House of Marcus Lucretius, Pompeii. 
The Latin name Cupīdō refers to earnest or passionate desire. Less romantic, the English word, cupidity, derives from the same root.
🏛 Naples Archaeological Museum 
🏺Ancientbluesky

Thonis-Heraklion was founded in C8thBCE at the Nile’s mouth. Before Alexandria’s creation, it was the major port of the delta. Succeeding earthquakes & floods saw the city slip under the waves in C2ndBCE eventually disappearing c.900CE. It has been excavated since 1933
📷Franck Goddio🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Golden Hat of Berlin is one of 4 found in Europe, all from the Bronze Age, and is also the best preserved hat among them.  These hats are actually astronomical calendars with which the seasons were calculated for the benefit of agricultural production. 🏺🏺🏺

10th century BC, Germany.

To mark the winter solstice, a 1st century Roman sundial from Baelo Claudia (Bolonia, Cádiz), given a monumental backdrop at Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid. 
Sunlight enters through the pinhole opening in the small dome. Eleven lines mark the hours.
🏺 AncientBlueSky
3rd century Roman mosaic bath sign depicts bathing sandals, curved strigils (to scrape off dirt, oil & sweat) & notice saying SALVOM LAVISSE (“bathing is good for you”) Roman Museum Sabratha, Libya

MosaicMonday, a panel from a larger mosaic depicting Odysseus escaping from Polyphemus by clinging to the underside of a ram. Dated to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE. Found in Rome, now in the Museo Nazionale Romano, Palazzo Massimo alle Terme.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Chepstow Castle, Monmouthshire is the oldest surviving post-Roman stone fortification in Britain. Located above cliffs on the River Wye, construction began in 1067 under the instruction of the Norman Lord William FitzOsbern. 

It was the southernmost of a chain of castles built in the Welsh Marches.

The most common perception of Classical sculpture - popular in fascist profile pics - is the kind of idealized human form found in Polykleitos. But by the Hellenistic era sculptors were exploring all kinds of bodies - including those marked by poverty, age, and combat (as here)

It’s #AncientSiteSunday! Today, Trier (or Augusta Treverorum) in western Germany. Here the Porta Nigra (started 170), the Aula Palatina (early 4th century), the imperial baths (also early 4th century) & the amphitheatre (mid-2nd century). 

Incredible remains.

🏺 AncientBlueSky

Porphyry pieces, including slab of an imperial Byzantine sarcophagus with cross, in the courtyard of Süleymaniye Mosque, which was built by the Ottoman architect Mimar Sinan in the 1550s. Porphyry, a purple stone from Egypt, stopped being quarried around the 450s.

El Infiernito (Spanish for "Little hell"), is a pre-Columbian Muisca site in Columbia! It is composed of several earthworks, standing stones and burial mounds are also present. The site was a center of religious ceremonies and probably also served as a rudimentary astronomical observatory. 🏺More 1/2

Prob the best studied climate-related range expansion of any European #birds is the Cetti’s Warbler. This skulking brown bird is the sole European representative of a largely Asian genus & is a resident species of reedbeds & wet scrub. 1/N 🌿🌏 🧪

Red-capped Cardinals can be found broadly throughout the Amazon river basin. They belong to a genus of tanagers, not cardinals, because apparently we like common names for birds to be confusing. 🪶 🧪

If you’ve never heard of His Master’s Voice, by Stanisław Lem, it’s probably one of my favourite works of science fiction, mostly because it’s just a carefully-written work of philosophy of science with absolute bangers on every page. I’d call it epistemic cosmic horror

Hurricane Lee is the 3rd fastest-intensifying hurricane ever observed in the Atlantic; and that’s no surprise.

Climate change isn’t making hurricanes more frequent, but it’s making them more dangerous as warmer waters power stronger storms that intensify faster, move slower, and dump more rain.

Most investments in electrification in Europe are now deflationary compared with fossil fuel alternatives, according to Goldman Sachs Research.

"Switching to electric heating and electric mobility would lower the overall [household] energy bill by more than 50%."

A new Oxfam report has found that the richest 1% of humanity is responsible for more carbon emissions than the poorest 66%. 

This “polluter elite” accounted for 16% of all CO2 emissions in 2019—enough to cause 1.3 million heat-related deaths over the coming decades.

In advanced economies, energy-sector emissions are now back to their level of fifty years ago. 

Advanced economy coal demand, driven by evolutions in the G7, is back down to the level of around 1900. 

- IEA

This dish isn’t terribly photogenic, which is a shame because it’s seriously delicious so I hope you’ll give it a chance. It’s a Greek dish called gigantes plaki. Dried lima beans are baked in a garlicky tomato sauce until they are velvety soft and divinely flavorful.

Back on my savoury porridge bullshit and can confirm that making the porridge with miso soup paste and stirring through some peanut rayu is probably the best idea I'll have in 2024. This one is topped with kimchi fried tofu and soft boiled egg and is the lunch of champions.

A lovely old French iris from 1927, 'Floridor'. It has a wonderful, heavy grape fragrance, but its best feature is the unique shade of blue, often described as slate blue in the old catalogs. It really stands out in the garden and is such a good grower and bloomer.

Born in the tropical forests of Sri Lanka True (Ceylon) Cinnamon is well suited to grow in our zone 9b. This tree can grow up to 50 ft, or be pruned to live well in a container. It will not tolerate temperatures below 40 degrees, be sure to protect it from rare frosts. 🌱

Image via Tanria Elementals

i don’t think it’s a huge exaggeration to say that the ‘shortage’ of funding for higher education (like other funding shortages) is a political not an economic reality, the result of a wildly successful propaganda campaign by the anti-keynesianism of a bunch of think tanks funded by billionaires

These stories never tell you what is actually happening, which is emphatically NOT that humanities are disappearing from higher ed, but rather that public ed is being converted into vocational training for a working class while elite private universities for the rich will remain universities.

I have often wondered why bullying is so common in academia. Is it because it is so dependent on personal relationships,  patronage and reputation. Or because it is normalized in its everyday functioning? Or because it is deeply hierarchical under its liberal facade? I think all of it is true.

A friend had a cat go missing four years ago and nobody wanted to crush his hope, but we kind of figured the cat had died.

The cat walked in the back door and demanded food last night like no time had passed at all.

Which is the most cat thing I've ever heard.

Reminder that while cats are amazing, no cat should ever be an outdoor cat. They are the most invasive non human species on earth, kill billions of birds and small mammals a year, and are second only to humans in number of species they've driven to extinction.

Aces just bonked his head on the coffee table hard enough that I'm pretty sure he lost ten IQ points, which he absolutely can't afford. Can you Nerf your cat?
(note: He's fine, just annoyed that I have furniture)

Messier 82- Some galaxies are especially good at generating bright new stars and have so much galactic dust and gas that they create stars  at a furious rate. M82 is also called the “Cigar galaxy” due the red glow of Hydrogen “smoke” emanating from its galactic core.Imaged from my Ottawa backyard.

The Orion Nebula (right) is about 24 light years across but only has a total mass of ~2,000 suns.

Most of the material is spread out over this vast distance making the gas very thinned out. 

That’s why (even it’s the size of two full moons) you can’t see it with naked eyes, it’s very dim. 🔭🧪

The Mercury-Atlas 6 mission launched OTD in 1962. John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth, circling three times in the Friendship 7 capsule. 

Glenn refused to trust the computer controlled trajectory until mathematician Katherine Johnson had checked it by hand. 👩🏾‍🔬 🔭 

Credit: NASA

The Pioneer 10 and Pioneer 11, launched in 1972 and 1973 respectively, included gold-anodized aluminum plaques with a message drafted by Carl Sagan in the language of astrophysics--directions for how to find us, and what we look like, should these strange shouts in the dark ever reach another world.

Astronomers: what say you? Have the Voyager spacecrafts “left the solar system”? We know they have passed beyond the heliopause, but what defines the edge of the solar system? 
(Isn’t the Oort Cloud still part of the solar system?!) 🔭

Just a quick 🧪🛰️ note about yesterday’s Chandrayaan-3 landing by ISRO. It officially includes the first seismometer on the Moon since Apollo. It’s a little high-frequency micromachined silicon sensor, which I think has a resonance up near 200 Hz, but it is a seismometer on the Moon!

A team of Malaysian researchers published a fascinating study of why gamification is a failure: essentially, shallow designs with meaningless rewards don't work as incentives, and slapping gamified elements onto something does not create a compelling experience. #CommSky

“Physics engines are incredibly powerful. Why isn’t it used in World of Warcraft or League of Legends?”

My answer:
* Multiplayer games are incredibly expensive to run
* Physics is expensive to calculate
* Keeping all clients in sync with the combination of the two is  incredibly difficult

A bit of a #Roman glass rarity. A spoon. 

Said to have been found near the Sea of Galilee, it was made from a single blown tube that was then tooled to shape the bowl and handle. Rare, made for the luxury market during the Roman period, lasting into Islamic times. 🏺

#MetMuseum #ancientglass

Is this the oldest street in Ireland? The Bronze Age village at Corrstown, near Portrush, had a wide cobbled street up to 10m wide and 95m long, with houses on either side and entrances opening onto it. 🏺

I WALK ALONE is worth seeing for its blockbuster cast alone, but the gorgeous photography and high growing tension ain’t too shabby. It’s one of those icy, glossy, detached noir films that I don’t fall for quite as much as the grittier stuff; it’s still essential (albeit uneven) #Noirvember viewing.

Next up is the 1979 Throbbing Gristle album, 20 Jazz Funk Greats. The title is tongue in cheek as the music is a mix of industrial and experimental electropop. It's hard to describe, but it's exciting, wildly influential, and a noisy work of post-punk art. Vinyl A to Z Summer spins on!

The Concertgebouw Orchestra, Amsterdam, played its first concert on 3 November 1888, almost 7 months after the Concertgebouw Hall opened. Since then it has developed into one of the greatest musical ensembles on the planet. What are your favourite Concertgebouw recordings?

Stacked Roman amphora fragments at Monte Testaccio in #Rome, essentially an ancient dumping ground for unwanted vessels (reusing not being a notable phenomenon) - these are just a small number of up to 53 MILLION vessels abandoned here in antiquity!

Remarkably well-preserved 4th-century Byzantine mosaic from ruins of a villa in Mariamin, Syria, depicts women musicians playing organ, double flute, lyre, forked cymbals, and an eight-bowl xylophone-ish instrument. Two young boys pose as cherubs at far left

This is the so-called Coppergate Helmet, named after its find location in York. It’s Anglo-Saxon & one of only 6 helmets surviving from that period. If you look at the nose guard, it has wonderful fantastical animals weaving through the decoration

🏛️Yorkshire Museum
📷Museum Trust

I come before you again with more adulations for the veggie burger at Ted’s Montana Grill. 
It’s vegan, though I am not, and the flavor combinations in this thing are incredible.
Herbs, spices, and a soft texture that is moist, but not greasy.

Scrambled eggs! Scrambled eggs! 🍳 This cute native wildflower is often called “scrambled eggs” or “yellow Corydalis.” Across their native range you can find them blooming in and around rocky areas, creek beds, and hillsides from late winter through early fall as temperatures and conditions allow! 🌿

Is there a point at which we stop calling the Great Red Spot "great"? It's still substantially bigger than any other persistent storm on Jupiter, but this is a pic from this past Tuesday. East-to-west, it's just barely bigger than Earth's diameter. 🔭🧪

The thing about having a cat is

when you hear a strange sound in your house, you don’t bother to get up because it’s the cat, but

When our Sun dies (a long time from now) it will turn into a white dwarf and consume some planets in the process. 

This paper reports a unique scar, the metal signature of a former planetary body on a white dwarf that rotates into view as the star rotates. 

The white dwarf has fed upon it.

🔭🧪

Santuario Madonna della Corona is a sanctuary in the Italian Alps near Lake Garda. It sits on a sheer cliffside, offering stunning views and housing a revered Black Madonna statue. Pilgrims visit for its tranquil atmosphere and scenic pilgrimage path, drawn by its rich history and natural beauty.

The Cloth of St Gereon is the oldest known European tapestry still existing, dating to the early 11th century. Parts of it exist in a number of museums.  The tapestry formerly hung in the choir of St Gereon's Church, Cologne.

The Moundville Duck Bowl, a stunning example of Indigenous Mississippian culture and craft, carved from a single piece of stone. Discovered in the early 1900s in Alabama. Made in the 13th or 14th C. 📷 Moundville Archaeological Park

Twelve Roman amphorae lost in a shipwreck off the coast of the Mediterranean island of Gozo 2100+ years ago; they then lay on the seabed until underwater archaeology brought them to the surface in 1961.

Near Naples, in Calvizzano, the superintendence has just discovered a superb hypogeum tomb imitating a banquet hall. I've pasted three screenshots to give a clearer idea. Behind the intact doorway, three stone beds with paintings immortalizing fabrics, and funerary furniture in place 🏺AncientBlueSky

This is a crackerjack example of ancient Egyptian glassware. This is an opaque, polychrome glass bottle in the shape of a ‘bulti’ fish. It dates from the 18th dynasty so it’s roughly mid-2nd millennium BCE. 

🏛️📷 British Museum 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Thamugadi in Algeria was designed to be home to 10,000 retired legionaries, but soon outgrew its original allocation.Because of the remarkable state of preservation of the ruins & the unique example of Roman urban planning with clearly delineated grids, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site

A series of camels carved into desert rock faces in north-western Saudi Arabia are actually prehistoric, dating from 7,000-8,000 years ago - before either the Pyramids of Giza or Stonehenge were built.

The cathedral of St Mary, Limerick, is also home to the only complete set of misericords to survive from medieval Ireland. These beautifully carved hinged oak seats, dating from the 15th century, were designed to give support to clergy who had to stand during long services.

Book bag under one arm, squirming Christ child under the other ~ carved by an unknown master from a single block of limestone, this is the 3/4 life-size polychrome Nostre Dame de Grasse, dating from late-15th century (photo: Nick Thompson; Musée des Augustins, Toulouse)

Bologna's leaning tower: The Garisenda Tower in Bologna is not as famous as the Tower of Pisa, but it leans a little more. Lately, though, the dynamic of its movement has become worrisome, and city officials have closed a city-centre square as they study what to do about the tower.

Enthralled by this stained glass from the cathedral at Le Mans showing the Virgin Mary whacking a man over the head with a hammer. 

Adams' "Mont-St-Michel & Chartres" says it depicts a miracle story told in the Maine about Mary, but I haven't been able to trace it further—anyone know? #medievalsky

Did you know that ancient Egypt had porn? (kinda) Turin Erotic Papyrus, ca 1150 BC, found at Deir el Medina (Luxor), shows 12 vignettes, the women are beautiful+ the men have facial hair+ bellies with enormous penises (sound familiar?). This was NOT normal for ancient Egypt.
Sometimes I think about how furry art is older than agriculture. This is the Lion-man. It’s 30 to 40 thousand years old, carved out of mammoth ivory and is the oldest known statue. Period.

Revisiting the intricately-carved stone latticework of ‘The Tree of Life’ in the window of 16th-century Sidi Saiyyed Mosque in Ahmedabad, India, named after the Ethiopian nobleman (& ex-slave) who commissioned it

Chartres cathedral, constructed within a generation after a fire in 1194, is rightly famous for its stained glass windows. But after visiting today I think the exterior carvings deserve equal adulation - truly incredible sources for material culture around 1200. #medievalsky

Kourion, an ancient city on the southwestern coast of Cyprus, was settled by Greeks from Argos who arrived in the 12th century. 

This mosaic depicts 2 gladiators being separated by a referee, late 3rd century CE, from the House of the Gladiators, Kourion.

📷 DM Photograpy

Witnessing the vibrant colours of freshly uncovered frescoes in Pompeii is a privilege and joy that never fades.
Seeing the latest discovery of a mythological scene—Phrixus seated on a ram while his sister Helle drowns—in the context of the room it decorated is no exception. 🏺

Most of the historic ceramics that I've copied have a surface design. 18th C. British agateware is different, made of two clays wedged together. During the trimming process the marbling is revealed. I love how this one turned out! Sherds were recovered in St. Augustine, Florida .
#maniarchaeology 🏺
Three Roman mosaic flowers, laid within a mosaic in Rome about 1900 years ago - colourful & beautiful! The mosaic they're part of is now in display at the Palazzo Massimo museum in Rome.

Reliefs of battles, hunts, weddings, and legends on Roman sarcophagi are fine, but take a moment to admire the heads or masks carved at the corners (acroteria) of the lids. These are four of my favorites from the Capitoline Museums, Rome.
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A #Roman glass vessel in the shape of a mouse, with a widened tail pointing upwards as the neck of the vessel. It is made out of beautiful blue glass, with added decorations giving the mouse features - it is a lovely & unusual piece of ancient glass.

Two golden bowls or ceremonial caps from the Late Bronze Age (1000-850 BC), found in 1972 at Axtroki, in the municipality of Escoriaza, Gipuzkoa, Spain. Nicely displayed at the Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Antinous Mondragone is an extraordinary, well-preserved colossal head of Antinous that is believed to have been part of an acrolithic cult statue, a composite sculpture made of stone and wood.
Louvre Museum, Paris.

County Tyrone is tonight's feature in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

Lots of very cool monuments here, a few favourites include the remarkable stone circles and alignments at Beaghmore, Tullaghoge Fort and the ogham stone at Aghascrebagh (who's placename means the 'Field of the Writing').

Nazca figure
This polychrome figure is in remarkable shape for its age - around 1,800 years old!
The ceramic pre-Columbian spouted ceremonial vessel comes from the Nazca civilization, which flourished on the southern coast of Peru between 200 BCE and 600 CE.

#Art #Peru #Nazca

I’ve written before about the astonishing discoveries at Gobekli Tepe, at 12,000 years the world’s oldest megalithic site and one that is completely rewriting what we know about prehistory. They’re still excavating, still turning up more and more head-spinning stuff, like this statue…

This incredible bronze cuirass was found in Maresse, France. It was made as the Bronze Age was transitioning to the Iron Age & dates to the C9th/8thBCE. Along with several others, it was given as a votive offering. Give it a whopping wow, please 

🏛️📷 National Archeological Musem 🇫🇷
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A Roman iron trident-head, found during excavations in central London. It may have been used by a gladiator who fought in the Londinium amphitheatre, which is just a mile away from the find site - if so, a retiarius gladiator, who fought with a trident & net AncientBlueSky

Layers of history: here we see a fancy mosaic floor that graced a large room in a large Roman building which now resides 2-3 feet below the paving of Chichester Cathedral’s choir ambulatory. 

📷mine

County Longford is next in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

A small (and often overlooked) county, but there are some really interesting sites. The Aughnacliffe Dolmen is perhaps my favourite portal tomb, the Cistercian ruins of Abbeylara Monastery, and the remarkable Corlea Trackway from 148 BC.
A beautiful Hellenistic/Roman-period glass pomegranate, which survives in amazing condition given that it is at least 2000 years old. A very lovely thing indeed! (Photo: Phoenix Ancient Arts)

It's WorldToiletDay today, and I've always had an appreciation for Roman latrines passed down from my MA advisor. Perhaps my favorite latrines from this summer at Aquinum (Aquino); all original, no reconstruction.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

A bronze of Hadrian found in England. Any bronze statue surviving antiquity is rare, but in England rarer still. It’s an interesting piece - visually striking with penetrating eyes & purposeful mouth, but rudimentary is some ways, eg the overly long neck, the low forehead

The Countdown to St. Patrick's Day.  This is the Giant's Causeway in County Antrim.   It's an area with 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, created over 50-million years ago. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.   The album cover for Led Zeppelin's "Houses of the Holy” was photographed here.

Researchers were able to demonstrate on the basis of the discovery of a more than 35,000-year-old perforated baton made from mammoth ivory at Hohle Fels Cave in Germany together with experimental studies, a way people of the early Upper Paleolithic manufactured rope
phys.org/news/2024-02...

Last month, the Maison Carrée (square house in French) was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site. 

It was inaugurated in 5 AD in the Forum of Nemausus (Nîmes) as a temple to Gaius and Lucius Caesar, grandsons & heirs of Augustus.

📷Jeff Wiehl

🏺Ancientbluesky

Agetria (Hodegetria) is a small domed Byzantine church built by cliffs on the coast of Inner Mani, a region in Southern Greece. The small peninsula of Tigani visible to the north was probably the site of an important Frankish castle in the 13th century.

Though not much survives, the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus must have been a literally awesome sight. 

Here’s a reconstruction from National Geographic showing the whole city (left) and a closer view from DK. 

I also attach pics of 2 lions from the BM that decorated it (📷 mine)

The landscapes of central and northern Germany were once full of megalithic graves. Many have been destroyed to produce material for road construction in the 19th century. Lübbensteine, near Helmstedt in Lower Saxony, Germany, ca. 3500 BCE. #Archaeology🏺

And another great discovery: archaeologists have unearthed a 6000- year-old Neolithic cursus- a ritual space - on the Isle of Arran, the only complete one to be found in the UK.

It stands close to the stone circles of Machrie Moor, an important ceremonial site.

Saw this in Berlin in an excellent exhibition on miniatures and polychromy. It is a miniature shrine with statues of Hermes and two nymphs on a base from Tanagra in Boiotia (c.400-350 BC)! 

The roof is missing, but it would have finished off this Polly(theism) Pocket set.
🦔 Ancient Cycladic hedgehog! This little guy is from Chalandriani, Syros, Greece. He dates to the Early Cycladic II period (2800-2300 BCE). National Archaeological Museum, Athens, Greece. 📷 My own.

"I have come from the City. I bring you a welcome gift with a sharp point that you may remember me," says a Roman stylus found in London, most likely brought there from Rome. 

Tourism and souvenirs - it was all there... 

arstechnica.com/science/2019...

The Rotunda of Thessaloniki is is usually identified as the Mausoleum of Galerius, who made Thessaloniki an imperial residence around 300, though it has also been attributed to Constantine. It was converted into a church between the late 4th and the early 6th century.

This astonishing landscape portrays Perseus rescuing Andromeda from ketos, a sea monster. It was found at the imperial villa of Agrippa Posthumus (murdered on the orders of Tiberius at his accession). It’s a bit different from the version in Clash of the Titans. 

🏛️📷NY Met
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Yes, lots of people died at Pompeii and Herculaneum when Vesuvius erupted in A.D. 79 - but what about the far greater number of people who survived? Where did they go?

Fascinating archaeology article about how people moved on after major disasters in the ancient world:

Thermopolium of Lucius Vetutius Placidus, Pompeii. 

The ancient equivalent of the fast-food restaurant takes its name from the Greek θερμοπώλιον to describe a place where hot food is sold. In the fresco, the food is being watched over by the spirit of the house. 

Ancientbluesky 
📷 Lauren Heilman

Belt buckles and bracelets made from Spondylus shells, a species living in the Mediterranean / Black Sea in depths around 15m, were between the favorite ornaments of the Linear Pottery Culture in Central Europe between 5500-4800 BCE. Historisches Museum Regensburg. #Archaeology 🏺

Carved stele inside the Table des Marchands, a Neolithic tomb at Locmariaquer, Brittany. The shapes may represent shepherds’ crooks.

📷Personal photo, July, 2023

#OnThisDay - 30 October - in 1829 the House of Meleager, Pompeii (VI.9.2), was excavated and offered up some truly staggering painted stucco reliefs. Imagine eyes being cast on this for the first time in 1750 years. #Pompeii

Jewelry and a set of jeweler's tools were found stored in a pottery jug in Arad, Israel. The materials include shell, faience, bone, copper, and flint.  From the Early Bronze Age, c. 3000-2650 BCE.
At the Israel Museum, Jerusalem.

The faces of Palmyra. These are tomb portraits of Palmyran elites dating from c50-270CE. The sculptural style, as well as that of clothes and hair, show a natural mix of Roman & Syrian influences. But look at those eyes - they’re looking at you from depths of the past
