Clay lamps may be some of my favourite Roman artefacts! This one's a little cracker, decorated with a fine scene of Odysseus, strapped to his mast, sailing past the Sirens: ca. Late 2nd Century AD. #Myth #Archaeology 🏺

Image: BnF (inv.65.5296). Link - medaillesetantiques.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/c...

I was in Abernethy last week to see @@504790 give a talk, which gave me the chance to visit and climb the town's amazing early medieval round tower. Built around 1100, it is one of only two in Scotland, although there are several in Ireland. Hard work, but worth it for the view!

This in Malmesbury (Wilts) is apparently the oldest inhabited building in England; converted into a house in the 1600s, it was originally the Saxon St Helen's Chapel, constructed around 900 AD. The large corner quoin (bottom right) is probably recycled from a Roman building.

The mosaic floor of the Alytarchus Stoa of Ephesus for this MosaicMonday. To the left is the protective roof of the terrace houses, to the right is the arch of Hadrian's Temple. Where people are walking on is the Curetes Street, one of the main streets of Ephesus.🙂🏺

For EpigraphyTuesday, a bronze helmet with a Greek inscription dedicating it to Persephone by Xena. Dated to the early 5th century BCE. Found in Locri, possibly from a Persephoneion in Manella. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Koi Krylgan Kala is an archeological site from Karakalpakstan Republic. Built by Iranian people from the kingdom of Khwarezm (a sister civilization of a perhaps more famous Sogdia) in 4 cent BCE. Koi Krylgan Kala was both a fortress, a settlement, and a Zoroastrian temple.

March in Cambridgeshire is well known for its Angel roof, however, set into the wall of the church hall opposite the church is this C17 relief that Pevsner suggests is a piece from a monument representing the Last Judgement. A pyramid of souls between two angels. A tad unsettling perhaps?

My scan Kingston Peak diamictite block on an alluvial fan. Block preserves evidence of snowball earth ~650Ma! Pebbles/cobble were trapped in bottom of glaciers that broke-into icebergs that floated to equator, melted & dropped stones and sediment onto/into seafloor sed

Every archaeological museum in Sicily is heaving with statues and other representations of Demeter and her daughter, Persephone. It shows how much corn growing meant to them. 

This polychrome example is from Syracuse &  is quite delightful

New OA paper by my team (lead: PhD student Silvia Gazzo) presenting taphonomic & archeological data to explain the presence of microshells (specifically Bittium) in Paleolithic sites, with Riparo Bombrini (Liguria, Italy) as a case study. We confirm their introduction was prob unintentional BUT... 🧪

I love the depth of his fresco. It feels like you’re actually standing in an orchard and could pluck one of those succulents fruits and gobble it up. It’s from the villa of Livia, Augustus’ wife, and is just one of many beautiful frescoes found there. 

🏛️📷 Palazzo Massimo, Rome
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Especially for @@42425, an ancient arch inside the church of St Andrew's, Corbridge. The building is mostly medieval, with a bit of Saxon, but this huge arch is all Roman. It was lifted from the ruins of the Roman town nearby and rebuilt here, a nice example of early recycling.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

The Crosby Garrett Helmet is an extremely fine Roman cavalry helmet dating from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD. It consists of a face mask and a headpiece in the shape of a Phrygian cap topped by a griffin. It was discovered by a metal detectorist in Cumbria (UK) in 2010.

A selection of Roman glass vessels excavated from sites on the island of Malta; many of then would have been used to hold perfume or scented oil. They're quite simple in design, but still very striking, nearly 2000 years after they were originally made!

I love this book. Though written in the 70s, its scholarship holds up. Horse collars, invented in China, reached Europe by the 900s and revolutionized agriculture.

The very first picture *anywhere* of a horse pulling a plough comes from the 11th C Bayeux Tapestry!

#medievalsky

The black-headed gull is a small gull that breeds in much of the Palearctic including Europe and also in coastal eastern Canada. Most of the population is migratory and winters further south, but some birds reside in the milder westernmost areas of Europe

The flame bowerbird is one of the most brilliantly colored bowerbirds. The male is a medium-sized bird, up to 25 cm long, with flame orange and golden yellow plumage, elongated neck plumes, and a yellow-tipped black tail. It builds an "avenue-type" bower with two side walls of sticks.

Though I know it’s the most colorful, flashy birds that usually draw us into birding, I’ve come to be more interested in the subtleties that distinguish diverse but sometimes overlooked groups, like sparrows. Here, though soon to be renamed, a juvenile Harris’ Sparrow in Bixby, OK USA. 🪶

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

This handsome little scooter is a Cinnamon Flycatcher. They call the Andean cloud forest home and can be found from Venezuela south to Chile. Like most flycatchers you can see them sallying from a perch to catch flying insects. Montezuma Rainforest Lodge, Colombia 🪶🧪🌿

🪶🦉Rose-ringed parakeet, enjoying some winter sunshine in an Antwerp park, where they established a feral colony in the last decade. They seem to perform assembly flights above the city at dusk, before roosting together. 
(halsbandparkiet, Psittacula krameri)

Hopalong is back! My one legged blackbird, not seen for months, reappeared in the garden this morning in his usual eating place, no longer molting, a full array of feathers. Now fat and full in his usual perch. Definitely him, same markings on his tail and by his left eye. This makes me so happy!

Pink cockatoo
The pink cockatoo, also known as Major Mitchell's cockatoo or Leadbeater's cockatoo, is a medium-sized cockatoo that inhabits arid and semi-arid inland areas across Australia, except the northeast.

A Grey Catbird I saw at MacGregor Point PP in Ontario.

Catbirds like to skulk around in the bushes so it was a treat to see one in the relative open. They are related to thrashers and mockingbirds, so as well as their namesake mewing, they have a vast repertoire of elaborate vocalizations.

Down on the flood plain in search of a rare visitor to these lakes. A morning of mud-splatter and cracked ice; of snipe flashing off the wet meadows and grey partridges thundering from thickets. And then there it was, the sudden immensity of a white-tailed sea eagle watching the winter waters.

This is Cassin's sparrow--a bird of the SW grasslands that, until yesterday, had never been recorded in the East Bay before. It was spotted at Albany Bulb in the am & several dozen happy birders went to see it. I LOVE how much communal joy a little brown bird (w/ a stunning voice) can generate. 🪶📷

I got to see this very photogenic lifer yesterday: a brilliant white Lark Sparrow that was hanging out in a flock of finches and bluebirds, foraging among the dew-covered grass at a local campground. 🪶

Frigatebirds have tiny little legs and feet. They can't land on water because they are unable to run for a takeoff roll like waterfowl. So what is a thirsty, dirty frigatebird to do? Crash and fly, my friends. Amazing to see this.
Floreana Island, Galapagos, 2018

Birding level reached: 🪶 Resplendent Quetzal 
This bird, a member of the trogon family, is one of the most extravagant natural users of metamaterials. Backlit, its feathers appear a dull gray, but when light reflects, they light up, traversing blue-green-golden depending on the angle. #wildlife
One thing I always love  about visiting the American tropics is seeing Trogons. Here's a Black-headed we saw at the Belize Zoo (Which is less a zoo than a wildlife rescue and sanctuary) #birding

Broad-billed motmot with a quite water-soaked feather dress.

Bet he was as surprised as me when the sudden rain shower started 🌧️

Spotting it was pure luck: Motmots usually wait motionless on branches until potential prey moves (insects).

Burrowing owls (Athene cunicularia) are found throughout the Americas wherever vegetation is low, and soil permits burrows.

They're prevalent in the mountain states of Colorado, Texas and New Mexico, especially in area where prairie dogs create convenient burrows.

(pic: Wendy Miller)

When I see a hawk perching, it's usually very far away and 80 to 100 feet up.  This red-shouldered hawk plopped down on a branch that was less than 15 feet up and relatively close.  Huntley Meadows Park, Alexandria, Virginia, USA 30NOV2023. #birds

Pacific Golden Plover seen on the Kona Coast of Hawaii. 

Pacific Golden Plovers breed in the summer months in Siberia and Alaska, and migrate south to Asia, Australasia, and Pacific islands for much of the winter. The Hawaiian population is well adapted to humans and are common park birds.

#birds

A bit of direct comparison of two of the gnatcatcher species in coastal California

The blue-gray gnatcatcher, wider range throughout continental US, has extensive white on the outer tail feathers

CA gnatcatchers have limited range to coastal SoCal and Baja CA and show only narrow white edges

📷🪶

#BirdGlamour’s Halloween-colored birds continues with Black-vented Orioles! These blackbird relatives call Central & South America home but are occasionally spotted in AZ & TX. Black-vented Orioles love an open area with scattered trees. Their name comes from their black vent (aka bum) area. 🐡🧪

Anna's Hummingbird 
           Relatively large-headed and chunky. Gray and green overall, lacking buffy or orangey tones.Males have shocking magenta head.
 Expanding its range northward into British Columbia, even regular in southeast Alaska. Winters to southern Baja and locally elsewhere in Mexico

After a week of chasing Parakeets on my bike, I’ve finally located the East Oxford parakeet roost. The local flock of 35-40 birds all roost in conifers just off Church Cowley Road, opposite the cemetery. Now, is there a north Oxford roost too?

Few wildlife spectacles epitomise the brutality of the natural world quite like the sight of a black-headed gull, carrying a croissant the size of its head, being chased by eight herring gulls over Morrison’s car park.

🧪🐡A purple-banded sunbird (Cinnyris bifasciatus) photographed in Maasai Mara, Kenya. Sometimes called “Africa’s hummingbirds”, sunbirds fulfill the same niche or ecological role as their hummingbird counterparts. They are specialized nectarivores and important pollinators.

Bird Glamour’s Glamager series continues with Hepatic Tanagers! This southern US to Central American dark red bird loves a pine-oak forest. These birds are little studied, but we do know they eat insects, spiders, and small fruits. Lady Hepatic Tanagers do most of the nest building. 🐡

A pair of Trumpeter Swans nests (dangerously close) to a road ~10 minutes from my house; I usually drive by and check on the cygnets every couple days. These little fluffballs become the largest native waterfowl in North America in no-time flat - the adults are 6+ feet long and weigh over 25 lbs 🪶

Welcome to the #BirdGlamour Glamager series, starting with the Western Tanager from western North America! The Western Tanager is extra and DOES NOT get his red coloration from a diet rich in carotenoids like many birds do: their red comes from rhodoxanthin from their insect-rich diet. 🐡🐦

Surfbird, a shorebird finely adapted to foraging on rocky coastlines in the surf zone, photographed today on the edge of San Francisco Bay.  Love the tiny water droplets beaded up on its back.

#birds

On the Hunt - Northern Water Shrew 🦋 
The North American Water Shrew is our smallest diving mammal at only 14-16cm long. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting in the pitch black of rivers and ponds by using its long whiskers to feel and blowing air bubbles which it then inhales to smell for prey.

I spent a small amount of time watching pacific ladder-backed — I mean… Nuttall’s woodpeckers… mating

After he finishes, he stays a while and strokes the back of her head a few times before they leave 

Such an intimate behavior to witness 🥰🪶

A great white egret wading thought an irrigated rice field at the estuary of the river Ebro, Catalunya. Despite invasive human intervention, the delta still teams with life and remains one of the main stopovers for flock of birds migrating from Europe to Africa.
📷 #photography #photocatalunya

Collared Incas are snazzy little hummingbirds. Most of the time when you see them, they look like they are wearing a black tuxedo. But if you catch them in just the right light, you can see some green to their feathers. 🧪🌿🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

Canada jays are quintessential northern birds - omnivorous, with thick fluffy plumage. They don't migrate, and breed in late winter (Feb/March) before arrival of spring migrants to their boreal forest home 🪶

Wildlife in my local #OnePlaceStudy. Fifteen species (butterflies, birds, wildflowers, dragonflies, grey squirrel, a fungus) caught on camera over the last couple of years at Fawsley in Northamptonshire, where I study the dead and photograph the living!

In May 2022 4 Glossy Ibis arrived on the Hampshire coast. They were mobile at first eventually preferring floods along the River Meon. In March, right image, they were still in winter plumage but by April they’d attained full summer. They moved on in summer but are back again now. #birds 🪶#ukbirding

This morning on the Boston side of the river, by Mass Ave and Storrow, I saw a young female bald eagle swoop into the Charles and then a second later it was flying over me with a fish in its talons. I have trouble with earnestness, but seeing bald eagles in the middle of the city is so fucking cool.

Gray Catbird numbers are way down from last week. Most of the pokeweed, Virginia creeper, and wild grape vines have been pretty well stripped of their fruits. I suspect this may be one of the last ones I see until next spring. Safe travels! #birds #fall #migration 🪶

🧪Rough day for that fish 😅 this is the pied kingfisher (Ceryle rudis), a species of cerylid aka water kingfisher. Cerylid kingfishers are true to their name and are specialized fish eaters. Photographed in Lake Naivasha, Kenya. I didn’t even notice the fish in its mouth until I was editing later!

The eared grebe has a piercingly red eye — totally freaky. In the fall almost all of them fly to Mono Lake or Great Salt Lake and pig out on brine shrimp and alkali flies, losing most of their flight ability and doubling in weight. They migrate at night. #Birds

💙 GM BlueSky! 💙

By their 3rd year, male Blue Grosbeaks acquire their full adult blue plumage. Regardless of age, males in particular often raise their crown feathers slightly when stimulated, giving the appearance of a small crest. 

Often?? I've only caught this 'crown' once! 😁

📷 P. caerulea 🪶🌿

Another forest bird I was extremely fortunate to see on my Kaua'i trip just over a year ago was this Puaiohi, a very rare thrush endemic to the highest elevations of the island. In addition to its harsh raspy calls, Puaiohi sing a song that essentially consists of their name 🪶
Red-throated Ant-tanager is a striking bird. It growls from bushes as you walk by. This one is a male. The females are russet gold and have some red on the throat also. #birding Belize

🔊👂 Southern ground hornbills are quite vocal! These social birds can inflate their brightly-colored throat pouches to let out loud, booming calls. This is done to announce location or attract a mate.

📷: Zookeeper Taylor S

Superb owl photo my wife took in 2016 in Brian Piccolo Park near Fort Lauderdale. The park is full of nesting burrowing owls who have no problem living among the people using the park for recreation, it’s really an amazing thing to see.

This palm warbler was one of a pair I saw zipping along near ground level, gleaning food. They eat primarily insects but will also eat seeds and berries. They also nest on the ground. Very un-warblery warbler! 🪶

Just finished @@346220 's MAD SISTERS OF ESI and it's magnificent, a tale of tales wrapped in iridescent spacetime, an infinitely nested creation myth, a portal and a ship and a campfire woven of prose. Reminded me of Calvino, and Clarke's PIRANESI (but spacefaring).

Currently reading the late Michael Shea's MR. CANNYHARME, which went unpublished in his lifetime. But damn is it amazing, with exquisite writing, Lovecraftian menace, and a street-level tour of San Francisco's Mission District.
Reading my second-ever T. Kingfisher book, Thornhedge, and this might be the best thing I've ever read: "thorns die from the inside out, like priests"

Un des livres, si ce n'est le plus important que j'ai lu pour comprendre Wakefield et le mouvement antivax actuel. C'est limpide. Deer est un journaliste fantastique et son livre explique avec méthode que la plupart du temps, tout ce qui compte c'est la reconnaissance et le pognon. Pas les patients.

Recommended read: The Blue, Beautiful World by Karen Lord. Stellar example of SF exploring the human condition with insight and compassion in a dramatic tale of ordinary people trying to cope when their small planet is caught between powerful alien races who may decide humanity’s fate is irrelevant.

Starling House by Alix E. Harrow.

A gorgeous gothic novel with a haunted house at its center and metatextual footnotes. I love how this story explores how history is created and our understanding of the past, how the stories we tell become what's true.

I should have read this decades ago. Extraordinarily daring for its time, in its depiction of sexuality, age and feminism, it's also a brilliantly-paced, multi-viewpoint story, which races the reader along. Marvellous. It's early days, but it's probably my book of the year.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

Everyone stop scrolling and go buy THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS by @@64002 RIGHT NOW

Weird fantasy, bleak horror, the absurdism of religion, race, caste, bureaucracy, and capitalism. The loneliness of parental abuse. All told with Vajra's sharp and beautiful prose. One of my top reads this year.

Reading Skin Thief by Suzan Palumbo.  Propagating Peonies might be the best story about the deep ambivalence at the heart of Buddhist practice I’ve ever read. This book is so ridiculously good. Deeply embodied stories about the spiritual homelessness of being pulled between 2 worlds

I was always going to read Rachel Nuwer's MDMA book 
'I feel Love', but it really has exceeded my expectations. It's fascinating and informative, not only on the history and current state of psychedelic science as a whole, but on the nature of trauma. Warmly recommended.

Legends and Lattes by Travis Baldree is for everyone who's already living in the year 3000 when their OCs have retired from saving the world and finally opened up that quaint downtown cafe they've been dreaming about.
love reading Auden because you get sheer devastation like "ghosts must do again / what brings them pain," then the goofiest possible use of the word "sexy"

Currently reading an ARC of @@208442's A MASK OF FLIES, and let me tell you, this is thing is SOLID. Great pacing, dialogue, violence, remarkable sense of place. Perfectly poised between that sweet spot of crime and horror. SO GOOD. us.macmillan.com/books/978125...

DEAD SET is probably my least known novel. It’s about a young punk—Zoe—who discovers her dead dad’s soul on an LP in a strange record shop, but loses it, and must travel to the underworld to save him. Almost no one has heard of the book, but it means a lot to me.

I’ve spent the evening with Essex Hemphill’s 1992 poetry and prose collection, Ceremonies. Dealing with black masculinity and queerness, the AIDS epidemic, kink and sex, and essays on the problem of Robert Mapplethorpe and the joy of Paris is Burning. Highly recommend

The greatest living ghost story writer is surely Reggie Oliver, who not only writes well, but also in a variety of styles including pastiches. He’s quite remarkable. Much of his work can be purchased through Tartarus Press.

Having slept on it I'm confident saying that The Malazan Book of the Fallen is the best fantasy series I have ever read. Completely engrossed me from start to finish. I read ten books, 1200 pages a pop, in four months. I could not put them down. The easiest recommendation I've ever given. Read 'em!

I’m shifting my 2023 reading thread from Twitter with #50, which is Room Temperature by Nicholson Baker. Baker is the apotheosis of James’s writer “on whom nothing is lost” and he turns what should be a curse of King Midas proportions (Remember and be forced to share every detail!) into a blessing.

PINK SLIME by Fernanda Trías (Heather Cleary transl.) is a sombre ecological catastrophe novel. The narrator lives in a coastal city infested with a toxic form of algae. The fish have died, the rich have moved away, and the protagonist suffers through isolation, depression and a family drama.

After finishing BRIDGE by @@179489, I screamed out loud and then lay motionless, hugging the book. What a breathtaking, masterful, relentless tale of grief and messy mother-daughter relationships and multiverse-hopping parasites. Please read it.

“We have deluded ourselves into believing the myth that capitalism grew & prospered out of the Protestant ethic of hard work & sacrifice. The fact is capitalism was built on the exploitation & suffering of black slaves & continues to thrive on the exploitation of the poor both black & white.”
-MLK

Marx affords the "economic" sphere of life priority because his ultimate concern is with human self-creation, and in class societies, self-creation is limited by how daily life is organized, which in capitalist society is in accordance with the law of value. Marx is not deterministic, capitalism is.

In the US, moving from the first quintile to the third or fourth in earnings is often enough to break the chain of generational poverty, but it's nowhere near enough to remove the scars of having been there.

Capitalism ruins everything because it requires taking and taking, constant growth and consumption of finite resources. 

Games becoming shitty and adding microtransactions? Social media platforms adding native ads and "pro" upgrades? Planes shrinking seats but charging more? 

THAT is capitalism.

A corporation is an entity controlled by shareholders that exploits its assets to create maximum benefit for its collective owners.
A union is an entity controlled by its members to exploit their assets (time, intellect, and labor) to create maximum benefit for its collective members.
Capitalism is alright at incentivizing people to bring new products to market but wow does its incentivization scheme fail once those products achieve any sort of scaled success.

Of course, the real tragedy of Moby Dick is that Ahab does not show enough consideration to his fiduciary responsibility to maximize shareholder value. In this TED talk I will show how the problems of capitalism presented by Melville can only be solved by more capitalism 🐋

This type of petrol, laced with high levels of toxic substances, is banned in Europe. But major international oil traders, continue to sell this dirty fuel to West African countries.

An astonishing 7 of Africa’s 30 deadliest weather-related disasters since 1900 occurred in the past 2 years. This could well be a harbinger of the future, as higher vulnerability and more extreme weather events from climate change cause an increase in deadly disasters.

Despite the fact that 2023 was an excellent water year for California, the long-term record continues to show a dramatic increase in drought frequency and intensity -- one signal of human-caused climate change.
As climate change intensifies, a quarter of the world’s freshwater fish are at risk of extinction, according to a new assessment which found that the biggest threat to the species was pollution.

Good to see the Advertising Standards Authority of Ireland (ASAI) rule against companies making 100% renewable electricity claims when largely based on the purchase of Guarantees of Origin (GO) certificates rather than direct renewable energy investment that would displace fossil fuels.
Reducing methane emissions is one of the easiest climate actions. The Biden administration announced that it will fine oil and gas companies $900 to 1,500 on every ton of excess methane emitted.

The world does not actually subsidize fossil fuels by $7 trillion, and it is super misleading that the IMF continues to claim that it does. What happens is that the IMF calculates the (huge) externalities of fossil fuels and calls them an “implicit subsidy.” Actual subsidies are about $1.3 trillion.

The lake that allows the Panama Canal to function recorded the lowest water level ever for the start of a dry season this year, meaning fewer ships can pass through. Climate change has made rainfall patterns much less predictable.

The German debt brake is undermining subsidies to climate-friendly durable goods. The green transition requires major up-front investment. The subsidies would also likely coordinate private sector expectations on a greener future, and thus promote green investment beyond the direct subsidy effect.

The European Commission is finalizing a series of legislative proposals that would severely weaken environmental requirements for farmers.

The move flies in the face of advice by its top scientists that agriculture must become more sustainable or it will be decimated by climate change.

An AI-based analysis of 20 years of VicForests’ logging, researchers say, shows the scale of failed regeneration in Victoria’s state forests.

My new favorite way to make potatoes. Get the small variety.

Boil them for 15 minutes (start preheating yoir air fryer after 10).

Serve and enjoy.

Malagasy vocabulary:

Laoka: a side dish that’s served with rice.

A “normal” Malagasy meal is supposed to be rice with laoka. Many Malagasy people are willing to accept a meal with very little laoka if necessary, but a meal with not enough rice is simply Not a True Meal.

Lazy porridge recipe!

- 125g salted butter
- 250g oat groats
- 1L water
- 0.75L milk
- 1 tsp salt

1. Fry the oats in butter, salt
2. Pour in water, cook in rice cooker on "brown rice" setting
3. Pour in milk, leave on "keep warm" setting
4. Eat when chewy & delicious (~6hrs)

Hot sauce taxonomy:

Yellow: Caribbean; mustard, vinegar, pepper

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha defines the type, but is its most mid member

Cholula-Tapatio: Classic hot sauce, more pepper than vinegar, often with some garlic

Tabasco et al: mostly vinegar

Beliezian: Like Cholula/Tapatio, but with carrot

Saffron grows from bulbs / corms. They bloom in the Fall and produce 3 threads that you want to carefully hand harvest on flowering day. Dry in parchment paper and place in an airtight jar. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Siberian Iris Paprikash is one of the stranger Siberian Iris I have. The flower color is decidedly different. Goes well with other colors in the garden. Like other Iris relatives it prefers full sun and tolerates dry when established. Not picky about soil. 🌱

Once the primary 🌱 agricultural focus of the region, #Meghri #Armenia has has shifted away from Soviet-enforced pomegranate orchards to the more profitable (and perishable) persimmon.

Deprioritized, pomegranates still get packed - but no new orchards get planted. Legacy trees straggle along.

Callicarpa bodinieri var. giraldii 'Profusion', Verbenaceae. The species is from E to Western China. This is in a North Yorkshire garden. Even on a dull day in low light, the fruits are startling - almost unreal

Start with a small act of anti capitalism.

Next time you buy a tomato, save the seeds. The slimy stuff on the seed is a sprout inhibiter. Put the seeds onto a tissue or paper kitchen towel. Save it. Dry it. Stash it somewhere dark. 

Next spring, soak in water and place in dirt and grow tomatoes

Two years ago when we moved here, this hydrangea was one single branch with a couple leaves. I thought it would not make it. The following summer, it had a tiny bloom. This year it has exploded in size and blossomed beautifully. We will have to move it this fall as it is crowding the gate 🌱

Last year’s dwarf irises—among my very favourite plants—were a treasure. This year we learn whether they’ll perennialize in the spots I picked for them, but as insurance, we planted another fifty. There can never be too many! They take up no space; their post-bloom foliage is narrow and subtle 🌱

Nasturtiums are a must for any garden. Beautiful, unusual, and fully edible. The bees also go nuts for them.

Today's Gardening Advice: Certain wildflowers deserve to stay right in the woods.  Various orchids and trillium are difficult to establish in the average garden.  Orchids like the common lady slipper have symbiotic relationships with fungus.  Gardeners are too often tempted to try them and fail. 🌱

I love Trillium 🤍 seeing them means spring is coming. It’s also Wisconsin’s State Flower. Don’t pick them because doing so can disrupt the plant’s ability to sustain itself and it can take years to bloom again- once established they can live 100 years!

Today's Gardening Advice: Hemerocallis "Katahdin" is one of my favorite daylilies. Tall scapes, prolific flowers, and grows well. A Ron Valente introduction, it is cold hardy and named for a mountain in Maine. Not the most fancy flower, but a robust, dependable performer. 🌱

one underrated factor here is that the Ivy League makes its students feel very Important and Meaningful (in mostly good ways! it's harder and better teaching) than Oxbridge does, which sustains the belief in the best and the brightest, etc.

Two things can be true at once: 1) DEI is largely a corporate box checking exercise that has little to do with actually addressing structural racism and 2) it drives actual racists insane with anger.

The University of Florida cut all of its staff positions relating to diversity, equity and inclusion and shuttered the Office of the Chief Diversity Officer to comply with a state mandate, the school announced in a memo on Friday.

Here are two things I want people with permanent jobs to know about being a precarious academic:
1. I have never had pay rise. 
2. I lose money to work at present. 

Neither of these are uncommon.

Compelling link between low levels of HE provision & low productivity: "If...you were looking to bring limited investment capacity to bear in promoting productivity and growth in...“left-behind” areas, one of the very best things you could build is a new university that recruits locally."

The answer to every story about elite college admissions is to make sure every American has access to a low price high quality public education within a reasonable drive of their hometown. 

The wild thing is we mostly achieved this. Then destroyed it.

As you read these stories in the NYT and elsewhere decrying higher education and championing its demise, keep this in mind:

Wealthy elites will NEVER not send their kids to college. They just don’t want your kids going, too.

The upper stratum of university administration: deans, provosts, presidents and vice presidents etc, are a species of executive, not a species of professor. I think this fact may not be visible from the outside. Often as not, they are even recruited from the private sector.

An Ivy League university bouncing a Nobel Prize winning scientist who in a very real way saved the world because, in their own fume-huffing opinion, she didn't do faculty level research, contains multitudes of indictments of elite institutions, funding agencies, and academia as a whole.

New AAUP survey of faculty in FL, GA, NC, & TX:

-2/3 would not recommend their state to colleagues as a desirable place to work

-1/3 are actively seeking academic jobs elsewhere 

-1 in 5 have already interviewed for jobs in other states since 2021

If you invest in language learning, linking with immigrant communities and employers and showing students the value of multilingualism, programs thrive. If you cut the tenure lines and the advanced courses, they shrink. news.mla.hcommons.org/2023/11/15/n...

An attitude I hear all over higher ed — that helping students understand skills gained in college turns liberal arts into voc-tech — is elitist on so many levels, from disdain for other routes to higher ed to the presumption that students have the luxury to just figure it out.

Hot take: administrative bloat is not the phenomenon of interest in higher education. Administrative professionalization is.  And insofar as this phenomenon is a problem, my academic field is a culprit in creating it.

“Low Major Enrollment” is the most grindingly obnoxious budgetary MacGuffin imaginable. Professors can be teaching 4-5 sections of courses in their field with every seat filled for years and it doesn’t necessarily mean students are *majoring* in that discipline.

There are many reasons to study the humanities but I don’t think it inherently makes you a better person. Like remember that we still have tenured philosophy profs out here publicly defending the right to sleep with their students

WVU is firing, firing 16% of all faculty. 

Sixteen. Percent. 

Because of 1) radical decline of state appropriation; 2) admin mismanagement leading to massive debt. 

Who pays the cost? STUDENTS and TEACHERS.

The Texas A&M Board of Regents directed the system's office of general counsel to conduct a “complete and thorough investigation” into what happened with Kathleen McElroy's job offer, and —this part is key — pledged to release the results to the public.

The board also authorized settlement talks.

One of the little-known aspects of American higher education is how much small colleges rely on athletics programs to keep their doors open and to enroll more men in particular. Take a few minutes and read this piece.

There’s so much wrong about higher ed right now, and a root cause of a lot of it is the bifurcation between actual working academics and the class of academics who jump on the administrative career track. 

The latter are a plague of one-size-fits-all theories and zero regard for local cultures.

So much breathless commentary on the role and worldview of the US Ivy League gets the global power dynamic around them backwards. 

They don't define the rise and fall of the US. Instead the influence of Ivy League universities rose with US global dominance and will wane as that power fades.

If two candidates are applying for a TT/permanent job and both have strong research profiles, but one has teaching experience and the other doesn't, I'm hiring the one with teaching experience, because that's at least 1/3 of the job & probably more.

(this is why no one puts me on hiring committees)

I bet many of us have sat in town halls and been told that cuts to staffing, compensation, and benefits, are necessary to secure "the future of the institution": something that always seems to be measured in buildings and pots of capital rather than the quality of future life it affords its people.

The sad fact is that simultaneously the primary benefit and core moral responsibility of being a tenured academic is not the production of “impact” or “influence” out in the world but rather the ability to look at something you know about that’s shite and say “this is shite” and not lose your job.
The fact that it's much easier to fire an elite university president than, say, an underperforming NFL offensive coordinator does tell you something about the nature of the role itself.

“If freed from the shackling dogma that their core function is job training, [universities] might do an even better job at what they actually do-namely, producing and embodying a reservoir of social/cultural/& technical capacities that enrich community life at every scale” slate.com/human-intere...

The demand for academic overproduction that’s grown along with the corporatization of the university is a scourge. It creates credential creep for junior scholars hoping to get a TT job, extra stress, and profits for the Wileys of the world—and little to nothing else 

dailynous.com/2023/05/01/t...

Perhaps unpopular take: 

university “40 under 40 awards” are ageist. I have some tremendous former students who switched careers or were non-traditional students and have done tremendous things since getting their degrees (and were over 40 when they got degrees).

What remains insane to me about university discourse is that unlike companies dependent on the market, governments set education funding. If a university is “failing” and cutting costs that’s a decision not an outside force.

just a heads up that when British academics say "admin staff" they usually mean professional services staff - often lower paid than academics, covering everything from cleaning, to IT support, to library staff. they do NOT mean, as US academics often do "highly paid senior management".

Throwback to that time a tenured law professor told me that he’d do everything in his power to prevent me from having a career in legal academia. 

I’m a law professor. What now, bitch?

I first went on the job market twelve years ago and am STILL explaining to people outside of academia that there are barely any permanent academic jobs, that the situation has been awful for decades and yet still getting worse, and the response is still "I don't believe things are as bad as you say"

Science academia keeps funding a ton of PhDs & post-docs (all temp labor) while its market of stable post-PhD jobs continues a long & steady collapse.

Proposal reviewers still go “Oh this proposal’s really great because it funds a ton of students!”

And no one reflects on that enormous imbalance.

I think most faculty in the humanities have been conditioned into a state of near-constant professional compromise, premised on a sense that it's the only way to keep doing what they're doing — which perhaps is true for individuals, but not great if you're trying to guarantee a disciplinary future.

Reading graduate applications and I have a great fear that we (disciplinarily, not just my department) are advancing only students who write well instead of teaching students to write well and this has really big implications for intellectual and social diversity in the academy.

This (from @@311753) may be even more true of medical ed

“Organizational scholar Joan Acker suggests that organizations are set up to benefit and meet the needs of a particular group of imagined organizational actors, actors she calls "ideal workers”…who reap organizational rewards”

RD-based estimates of Pell Grant effects often appear muted because students near the income threshold move in and out of eligibility over time.

That may makes the $ seem less effective than they are.

Great new work from Monnica Chan and Blake Heller.

edworkingpapers.com/ai23-851
I remember writing a DEI statement for a job at a school where I started out by throwing their abysmal diversity numbers in their face and essentially saying, “I am the diversity, what y’all doing?”

One way academia erodes academic freedom is by determining whose freedom counts - they can maintain some semblance of legitimacy by keeping a few tenured while casting the rest of us to contingent and staff roles where we do not have academic freedom.

Working in DEI, I am constantly asked by fellow white people, “But shouldn’t the most qualified person get the job?”
Yes. And ensuring that is exactly why we do this work.
The cuts to the liberal arts at WVU, especially those to the languages, will tell WV’s many rural, working-class, or first-generation college students not to get ideas above their station.

Just once, it would be nice if the higher ed sector acted like a sector, defending itself and undermining its opponents, rather than a debating society, arguing in public over whether its critics have a point. Amoral industries like oil and gas get this. Why doesn't higher ed?

Requiring graduate students to have extracurricular activities, leadership, etc when applying for grants.....

... really disadvantages lower SES students. Time and time again, I see great students "lowered" in rankings for reasons often beyond their control (e.g., worked retail, didn't volunteer)

One obvious thing that's become painfully palpable to me over the past few yrs: the academy feels fine + sustainable to those enjoy course releases + fellowships + manage to circumvent service. Those of us pulled into the unglamorous work of *sustaining* our institutions are burnt + spit out
Carpenter’s Halloween is a classic example of a movie that lots of people recall as being far more gory than it actually is. The Thing, on the other hand…

Just finished Poor Things (2023). I’ve seen most of Lanthimos’s previous films, and they were good, but I’ve had a bit of trouble connecting with them. I had no such issue here. This is marvelous stuff. Visually incredible, this is one of the best looking movies I’ve seen in a quite a while.

Mandabi (1968)

A biting, Kafkaesque tale about a poor man dealing with corrupt Senegalese bureaucracy

Directed by Ousmane Sembene, BOTD in 1923

enraptured by the lurid pink/green two-tone technicolor palette of Doctor X (1932).

directed by Michael Curtiz (Casablanca), this pre-code mad scientist horror movie was so clearly one of the many inspirations behind the Rocky Horror Picture Show.

such a beautiful & elegant minute-plus long crane shot in the funhouse. tracks the little brother making his way towards the exit as stragglers clear out. captures unspoken confusion of this kid by considering space around him. this kid's experience on this night is the best stuff in the film imo

This weekend's watch. SEX, LIES & VIDEOTAPE (1989).  Haven't seen it in 25 years at least. Forgot how good it is.

Made for a scant $1M, it won the grand prize at Cannes, & Spader won best actor. Brilliantly written. Marvelous characters.

If you haven't watched it in awhile, highest recommendation.

Wisconsin Death Trip (1999)

A very fine, very macabre adaptation of Michael Lesy's classic book of the same name.  Recommended for fans of folk horror.

Written and directed by James Marsh

Cinematography by Eigil Bryld, BOTD in 1971

Ram Charan - 📽️ Magadheera (S. S. Rajamouli, 2009)

Charan plays two roles: a street-bike racer and his past life ancestor, a legendary warrior in 1609. With distinct voices and demeanours, the mega-star of Tollywood, India embodies each of the ambitious epic hit film’s many genres and modes.

Dreamchild (1985)

A sadly neglected British fantasy film in which now elderly Alice Hargreaves, the inspiration for Lewis Carroll's Alice books, reflects on her childhood relationship with Carroll.

Screenplay by Dennis Potter

Directed by Gavin Millar, who was BOTD in 1938
Your annual reminder that Constantine is a bad Hellblazer movie, but it's one of the best overall paranormal procedural maybe ever made. It has no right being as visually inventive as it is.

Tonight I saw St Drogo. It’s low-budget, queer, slow-burn, folk horror. I loved the cinematography, and the interesting critical takes on status & community. I also think there’s a lot going on about the nature of desire, and the diverse ways it’s regulated by religion, money, and social groups.

ANTICHRIST (2009)
Dir. Lars von Trier
Denmark, Germany
Thriller, Horror, Avant-Garde, Drama

Grieving for the loss of their infant son, a couple attempt to repair their marriage by retreating to their remote woodland cabin. But as they descend into madness, a violent battle of the sexes emerges.

Bringing Out The Dead doesn't get its due, both as a Scorsese film and as a film capturing the spirit of the late 90s, with possibly Nicholas Cage's best performance. 

It's surprisingly gothic in atmosphere and manages to capture Scorsese's twin passions of New York grime and human spirituality.

MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. Love the absurd logic of Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins blackmailing Victor Mature and compelling him to take her to his country estate so that she can learn to be a lady.  Love even more that we are supposed to root for her and want her scheme to succeed.

NAKED LUNCH (1991)
Dir. David Cronenberg
Canada
Drama, Avant-Garde, Cult

A part-time exterminator who uses insecticides to get high who flees to a secret organization after accidentally killing his wife, plunging into a nightmarish interzone of sinister cabals and giant talking bugs.

Watching a V-Cinema action movie shot in New York, marquee spottings so far: The Return of Superfly, Marked for Death, Death Warrant, Tune in Tomorrow…, Jacob’s Ladder, Savini’s Night of the Living Dead remake, Prince’s Graffiti Bridge, Rocky V, and King of New York

Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat. One of the most platonically perfect movies I’ve ever seen. The dancing is at the  ‘impossible’ level of difficulty. One is reminded of watching Bruce Lee. That these two should also be great at screwball comedy boggles the mind.

Just rewatched Week-End in Havana (1941), which is getting to be one of my comfort movies. It’s one of my favorite Fox musicals… the songs aren’t too good (except for the 3 Carmen Miranda numbers), but it’s a wonderful screwball comedy. All the characters are great and the Technicolor is magnificent

Uncle Peckerhead is one of the best, most heartfelt / punk horror comedies in years, with really great original music, and i feel like a lot of people didn't give it a chance because it has an odd name. You should watch it!

watch Ravenous (1999) for a film that combines a sharp critique of manifest destiny, a suggestion that cannibalism somehow gives you super powers, deep homoeroticism and one of the wildest tones you've ever seen - all in one really weird movie

Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about a door-to-door salesman who is lured into crime by a teenage prostitute.  Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.  

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, who was BOTD in 1936

A few years ago, I came upon an excellent and suspenseful British hidden gem called THE APPPOINTMENT (1982). There was no hope for a re-release back then, but BFI did the impossible. Wrote some words about the film at Bloody Disgusting.

Didn't know what to expect from Doctor X (1932) but I was not prepared for a movie this luscious, creepy and funny. A two-toned marvel full of gloomy Gothic settings and Art Deco mad science in stunning, sickly Technicolor. A blast from Michael Curtiz (Casablanca!) in one of his rare horror forays.

A shockingly violent film, but also a very stylish/thrilling one. I won't even begin to pretend that this flick is in anything but bad taste.  But bad taste is what fans of a film like this want, and Takashi Harada grasps how far he can push the limits without descending into out and out vileness.

THEY CLONED TYRONE - provocative, satirical sci-fi with an exceptional cast that blends Blaxploitation and absurdist humor. Wildly entertaining and dripping with style and social commentary. A mix of THEY LIVE, GROUNDHOG'S DAY, and US. Boyega, Foxx, and Parris are FANTASTIC.

Fifty years today, arguably Ray Harryhausen’s magnum opus, and certainly one of my seminal texts, received its London release. A truly astonishing cast, a hugely evocative Miklós Rózsa score, an inventive screenplay, and some of cinema’s most monstrously beautiful creatures!

PARIS IS BURNING (1990)
Dir. Jennie Livingston
United States
Documentary, Queer
#DesertIslandSundance

Where does voguing come from, and what is throwing shade? This landmark documentary provides a vibrant snapshot of the 80s through the eyes of New York's Black and Latine Harlem drag-ball scene.

Just finished View to a Kill on the Bond rewatch. Not an objectively good Bond but so much fun after the chore of Octopussy. Roger Moore’s old man noises dubbed onto his stunt doubles are unintentionally hilarious. Walken & Jones having chemistry. Patrick Macnee there to make Moore seem younger.

Revisited Lake Placid (1999) this evening. Hot damn, that is a very 90’s movie. The dialogue belongs in a 90’s museum. Bill Pullman gives absolutely nothing to his role. But the CGI holds up surprisingly well, the crocodile still looks great! And of course very large. 🐊

Just watched Cry of the City (1948), a terrific noir film with Richard Conte as an escaped murderer trying to escape the city. Fast paced, hard dialogue, and exceedingly good performances by Conte and Victor Mature. Highly recommended for noir aficionados.

A friend & I have been working through notorious/reviled films. Last night was CANNIBAL FEROX (1981). These Italian cannibal films are fascinating. They follow virtually no narrative rules & stories exist in a pre-globalised international zero setting. The violence is full on, esp against animals

Palm Springs (2020) really impressed me. It's not the greatest film ever, but it is one of those rare movies that quickly established its tone and expectations before accomplishing exactly what it set out to accomplish in a tight 90 minutes. Plus, it was fun! 📽️

A film that’s haunted me since it’s release in 1990 is Jacob's Ladder. Vietnam vet Tim Robbins sees demons wherever he goes & has no idea why. The film's all practical special effects have had an outsized impact on pop culture, influencing everything from other movies to video games, eg Silent Hill

Watched a couple different rom-coms last night, My Best Girl from 1927 and No Hard Feelings from 2023. The formula  stays the same over a century — two mismatched people come together over a lie, they have some crazy fun, they fall for each other, the lie is revealed, but they get a happy ending.

Another one I love so very much that, like Ravenous, has finally started to get the love and attention it so richly deserves is Pontypool. It's such an odd little film. Shot almost entirely in a single location, with much if not all of the action taking place outside, offscreen. But it is so *TENSE*

Tonight's recommendation: TITANE (2021)

Alexia is a dancer who, after being injured in a car accident as a child, has a titanium plate fitted into her head. Amidst a series of brutal and unexplained murders, her path crosses with Vincent, a firefighter searching for his long-missing son.

THE EXORCIST hasn’t lost a shred of the sheer power it held over me as a kid. Horrific, vicious animal of a horror film that claws and bites at the soul. Will never lose its place as one of if not THE scariest films ever made

Portishead is one of the most remarkable bands of the 90’s - they helped define trip-hop but refused to be boxed in by it. Their short discography covers an insane amount of aesthetic ground, but their debut remains their most instantly accessible work. A perfect moody late 20th century soundscape

Continually blown away by Brahms's 4th. I have no doubt that it is one of the most moving symphonic descriptions of a life filled with fleeting ecstasies, joys, mournings, disappointments and everything in between ever written. What a piece, what a piece!

Jazz OTD: The brilliant album “Milestones” marked, appropriately, a pivotal moment in jazz history, with the end of Miles Davis’s first great quintet. Cannonball Adderley made it a sextet, and Red Garland walked out after recording four tracks, including this one, on February 4, 1958. Jazzsky

Jazz OTD: Cannonball Adderley’s leadership of the “Somethin’ Else” session on March 9, 1958 is a bit nuanced, given that the lead is often taken by his overqualified sideman, Miles Davis. But holy cow does he own this solo. A universally beloved album, deservedly so. jazzsky

The Breeders-Last Splash 30th anniversary edition. Cut from the original 1/2” tape and pressed across 2 LP’s at 45 RPM with a bonus disc with 2 additional tracks. Every pressing I’ve heard of this album was atrocious. THIS sounds amazing, though. Dynamic and full, finally.

Jazz OTD: Throughout the 1950s, Sonny Rollins seemed eager to bring a jazz edge to whatever sources he could, and on his brilliant Blue Note debut, "Sonny Rollins Vol. 1" (recorded December 16, 1956), he and Wynton Kelly have fun with "How Are Things in Glocca Mora" from "Finian's Rainbow." jazzsky

I am utterly blown away by John-Arne Ø. Gundersen and Anne Mette Hårdnes recording together as Sweetheart.

With the cuts to programs at state universities and community colleges, doctoral programs will become even more socio-economically homogenous. I was the only student in my PhD cohort from a state school. I remember the chuckles I received when I would tell folks I graduated from Boise State.

"Institutions with a historical record of bullying performed more poorly in the 2021 Research Excellence Framework (REF) exercise. Those with good management cultures performed much better."
21percent.org?p=785

It’s been said before but it’s absolutely wild that impact and success in academia doesn’t correlate with secure employment. I have contingent colleagues doing incredibly impactful work (more so than many TT folks) with zero institutional support. They’re also not afraid to speak up about injustice.

Tenured faculty are losing their jobs for financial reasons. Academic freedom protections are being gutted. 

The adjuncts and contingent faculty members *literally told you this would happen* and you didn't listen, didn't believe it could happen to you, because you were special, we weren't.

Deflection of starlight by the Sun’s gravitational field, measured during a solar eclipse and confirming a central prediction of Einstein's general relativity, was announced at a joint meeting of the Royal Society and the Royal Astronomical Society OTD in 1919. 🧪 🔭

In distant space outside our galaxy is... something big. Known as the Great Attractor, it's the gravitational centre of the Laniakea Supercluster, millions of times as massive as the Milky Way, and we can't see it. The galaxy itself is in the way! But gravitational waves aren't blocked by matter.

Just had a black hole explained in such simple terms (probably an over simplification)

The Earth's escape velocity is ~8 miles/second

Our Sun's is ~400 miles/s

A star can grow so massive that its escape velocity exceeds the speed of light. That's a black hole, light can't escape for us to see.

Fort Rock, a tuff ring about a mile in diameter, north of the town of the same name in central Oregon. Wave action in the pluvial lake here in the Pleistocene eroded it into the amphitheater shape we see today.⚒️

For #WorldBeardDay a rather wonderfully diverse take on ancient beards in ceramic, fresco, marble, and bronze mostly from around the Bay of Naples.

St. Kilmacrehy Church, Lahinch, Ireland.
Records start during the 14th century for this place, with a mysterious bone stealing monster (more than likely an otter or somesuch) carving hidden away near the back. This is why most of the graves here have slabs covering them.

Last year's record-high global sea surface temperatures hit coral reefs hard. Not too hot but not too cold is their comfort zone and stressful extremes can cause bleaching aka corals expel algae, lose their colour, and become vulnerable to starvation, disease, and even death 🌡️🧡 

📸: NASA/Landsat

Two years ago, I planted passion flower seeds, but they didn't germinate — due to weather conditions. One seed found its way to the base of a small tree and sprouted. Assuming I have academic freedom protections, which is not at all clear, I still can’t hurl racist slurs at my kid’s soccer game.

It is beginning to look like the probable result of Texas cracking down on college DEI programs and such is opening themselves up to a series of slam dunk lawsuits that cost them money and end up publicizing all of the emails and texts of the boards of regents and university executives

Passing Stars Shake Up Simulations of Earth's Past Orbit and Climate

Stars passing within a few light-years of our solar system add an element of chaos to planetary orbits, limiting how far back we can study Earth's past orbit. aasnova.org/2024/02/14/p... 🔭🧪

Voyager 2 was launched into deep space 46 years ago this month. It was expected to last 5 years, but is still going. Voyager 2 lost contact last month when the wrong command turned its antenna away from Earth. Today, its "heartbeat" signal was detected, so this amazing craft is still at work.
Astronomers call it a “Hot Jupiter.” WASP-69b, an exoplanet orbiting a star 160 light-years away, has a 350,000-mile-long tail of helium gas that billows out behind it like a comet’s.

"Sleeping Hermaphroditus," an ancient Greek marble sculpture, unearthed in Rome ca. 1600-1610, now in the Louvre. The mattress, by Bernini, was added in 1620.

A megalithic monument in Spain that's older than the Pyramids was recently uncovered from its watery hiding place by a drought. 

The "Spanish Stonehenge" is about 7,000 years old, some 2,000 years older than Stonehenge itself.

It's a year ago since I fulfilled a lifelong ambition to visit the Bronze Age site of Akrotiri on Santorini. It's a truly extraordinary place. Buildings destroyed in a volcanic eruption c. 1600 BC still stand, some up to 3 storeys high, many with their contents. 🏺

Syracuse, Sicily: one of my favourite Greek cities. Herewith, the Temple of Athena (built into the cathedral), the theatre, the vast altar of Hieronymous & Dionysius’ ear. Wonderful remains in one of the biggest, richest and most powerful Greek cities in the ancient world 
📷 me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple, meaning "the Great God of the Cave", is the largest and most ornate Hindu temple in the medieval temple group found at Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, India. It is considered one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India.

Longmen Grottoes in Henan Province, China. Over 2,300 caves filled with over 100,000 statues of all sizes. 4th-10th-centuries CE

This is a UNESCO-recognized World Heritage site, representing the pinnacle of Chinese stone carving art.
A sight to behold and over a thousand years old: the early 11th century Saxon tower (bottom two stages) at St. John the Baptist, Barnack, Cambridgeshire.
Eye-catching Roman mosaic thresholds that sit between the columns of the portico around the peristyle garden at Villa San Marco, Stabiae.

Crumbs this is special. It’s a glass model of a boat. It was found at Pompeii & was made in the 1st half of C1stCE. The craftsmanship & skill involved in making this is beyond my comprehension, but what’s truly arresting for me is the sleek shape and intense colouring. A marvel
🏛️📷BM 🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Roman Emperor Caligula had built two massive, elaborate palace-ships on Italy's Lake Nemi, the larger and more elaborate of which featured marble structures, mosaic floors, heating, plumbing, and a full Roman bath facility.

Chiricahua Nat. Monument (SE Arizona) has fabulous landscapes, views, and especially stone spires, pinnacles, and balanced and tumbled rocks, shaped by erosion after a cataclysmic volcanic eruption 27 million years ago covered the area with ash that hardened into rock. Gets remarkably few visitors!
Details of one of the T columns in Göbekli Tepe, Turkey. It is approximately 12,000 years old, making it remarkably older than Egyptian Pyramids and Stonehenge.

For FrescoFriday, from the triclinium of the House of Cornelius Diadumenus at Pompeii, a scene depicting the nymph Callisto (standing at left) and Artemis (seated with Eros). Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

The complex around Rathcroghan is one of Ireland’s most important archaeological landscapes, while Roscommon Castle may have been the prototype for the famous royal castles of Edward Ist.

San Lorenzo, Turin 1668-1687, Italian Baroque masterpiece designed by Guarino Guarini, architect & mathematician, born  #OTD 1624; known for his use of complex geometry & innovative structural engineering techniques.

House of Orpheus VI.14.20 #Pompeii named for the large painting of him playing his lyre surrounded by animals (that lion!). The painting in the peristyle was viewed through tablinum, classic sight-lines. Tons of amazing decor in this house much now sadly lost. #ClassicsTober23 day 23🏺

Two hazel wattle or hurdle structures, a Middle Bronze Age trackway at Annaghbeg, Co Longford, and a medieval fishweir at Boarland Rock, Fergus estuary, Co Clare; separated by thousands of years in time, and by thirty-four years of an archaeological career…

🏛 The Erechtheion on the acropolis of Athens.

🏛 Named after the mythical Athenian king Erechtheus.

An interesting find! It looks very much like it's the burial ground of the Premonstratensian abbey of Woodburn, a late 12th century foundation. Woodburn had links to the Anglo-Norman lord John de Courcy, who was himself the builder of the nearby Carrickfergus Castle.

medievalsky 🗃️

Part of the Valchitran Treasure, a hoard of gold vessels and discs from Bulgaria. Discovered in 1924, its date has been discussed ever since. Opinions based on stylistic comparisons range from the 2nd to the 1st Millennium BCE. Archaeological Museum Sofia. #Archaeology 🏺

A murmuration of brick in Wickham, Hampshire of 1648. This has to be one of my favourite brick buildings. A glorious attempt at a polite frontage in a vernacular building is made all the more divine by its idiosyncrasies. Full of personality.

So this is cool: Las Trampas Wilderness sits on a couple of fault lines + its mountains were formed by seismic and volcanic activity. These are shell fossils from when this area was part of the seashore 3-4 million years ago!!
Venus of Brassempouy is a 25,000 year old ivory figurine discovered in France and is possibly the oldest representation of the human face

A selection of beautiful Roman glass vessels, including several bowls in clear & mosaic glass, & a bottle shaped like a bunch of grapes. Together they are a wonderful testament to the skill of glassmakers in the Roman world! (photo: Corning Museum of Glass)

We want to share a unique artefact currently on display at Hollytrees Museum - this ‘charm’ isn't just any trinket; it's crafted from metal salvaged from the wrecks of German Zeppelin airships that met their fate in Essex.

A famous example of Ukrainian palace and estate architecture, the Galagan Palace in Sokyryntsi is a striking monument dating back to the 18th and 19th centuries.

The palace was built between 1823 and 1829, commissioned by Pavlo Halahan and designed by Pavlo Dubrovsky. ⬇️

An iron dagger was found in King Tut's tomb but the Egyptian iron age was 250 years after his death.
Outside of Egypt there was a small amount of iron work but it was only used for small jewelry and ceremonial items. It was worth more than gold.
One theory is the dagger was made out of a meteor.

One of my favourite mosaics from the Vesuvian area because of its simplicity and the curiously modern, stylised look of it.
From the caldarium in the bath complex at Villa Pisanella, Boscoreale now on display in the newly reopened Antiquarium at Boscoreale.

This magnificent miniature masterpiece is an early C3rdBCE tetradrachm of Lysimachos of Thrace. It depicts Alexander with the divine horns of Ammon & on the reverse Athena holding a tiny winged Nike (victory). This side also has the logo “of King Lysimachos.”

Was in Nantwich today, and uncle pointed out this gargoyle of a woman with her hand in a jar being carried off by the devil. Apparently the stonemasons caught their landlady stealing from them, and added this to the church in her honour. Still there nearly 700 years later. That’ll teach her.

The Great Barn at Harmondsworth is the largest standing medieval timber-framed structure in Britain.

Built in 1425, it's regarded as an outstanding example of medieval carpentry, built with the same skill and attention to detail as palaces.

Detail of the ‘dancing figures’ from the so-called Villa of Cicero at Pompeii. The fluidity of the poses, as though floating in the air, combined with the arresting diaphanous material of their dresses is simply stunning.

Bronze Age sewing was carried out with bone needles. These slender-headed needles with rounded or flat-topped heads are from the Late Bronze Age Hillfort at Dún Aonghasa, Inis Mór, Co. Galway. 🏺

The Roman wall surrounding the city of Lugo in Galicia is the only complete Roman wall remaining. According to legend the Romans built it to protect a forest and not a city, the“Sacred Forest of Augusto”. The walls still contain 85 towers and have survived intact for 18 centuries.
ancientbluesky 🏺

I love this. It’s a postcard of the temple at Abu Simbel from 1923. The photographer is William Goodyear. Don’t know about you but this positively reeks of a cross between Agatha Christie’s Death on the Nile and Karloff’s The Mummy. Also, that font…magnif. Art Deco-tastic!

One of the wonderful painted Romanesque capitals atop columns at the Church of Saint Austremoine d’Issoire, in France. The church dates back to the 12th century, and was restored in the 19th century arteviajero.com/articulos/sa...

The burnt out ruins of 13th century Holy Trinity Church ⛪️ stand high on the hill above the town of Buckfastleigh, #Devon. Destroyed by arson in 1992 — it’s an atmospheric place with many stories to tell.

The Hungarian Rocks, or stones, are a group of tall vertical cliffs located in the middle of a spruce forest in the Carpathians.

They are known for their unusual shape and are considered one of the least studied megalithic complexes in the Carpathians.⬇️

The Drususstein, on a beautiful blue day. The massive concrete core once clad in marble, set up to commemorate Drusus’ death by his troops in the  #Roman city of his founding, Mogantiacum (Mainz)

A fantastic cross section of an Athenian Trireme, aprox 450BC. I'm always struck by how vibrant the paint must have looked against the turquoise of the Mediterranean sea. The classical world was clearly wonderfully colourful. #maritimehistory #history

Medieval upper class dining equipment. An aquamanile, a vessel in the form of an animal or human figures. It contained water for washing the hands over a basin. #archaeology 🏺

Open water on the lakes and ponds around Anchorage #Alaska is getting pretty scarce but Common Goldeneyes can get by with surprisingly little water. A few usually manage to stick it out after most other ducks have bailed on us. 🪶

The Gold-ringed Tanager has the local common name Bangsia de Tatama for the very limited range where it is found. The tanager is the symbol of Tatama National Park and a species that has inspired conservation in Colombia. 🪶🌿

I've been fascinated with 🪶 Boat-billed Herons as long as I've known they existed, it was a privilege to observe this mating pair discussing suitable nesting sites this morning in Tortuguero National Park, Costa Rica

Black Phoebe. These birds have a long range - from the western US south all the way to Argentina. They are primarily insectivorous. If you love birds, gotta conserve bugs. 🌿🧪🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

Check out this really incredible example of xanthochroism in a warbler. This is a condition of excess yellow color deposition in the feathers, but in this case the naturally melanic patterns seem to be overriding the excess yellow, making for a gorgeous bird 🪶

Ladder-backed Woodpeckers (Dryobates scalaris) are native to the SW US and MX. They can be inconspicuous and quiet, requiring a bit of time and patience to find. Their small size and agility make them deft foragers.

Woodpecker Wednesday! 🪶🌿

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

Today's #101CuriousTales concerns the Woodpigeon, one of Europe's most numerous #birds. Such high numbers of a farmland bird makes these a major pest species. In the UK Woodpigeons are resident, but here & elsewhere in Europe huge numbers will soon be moving south.  1/N 🧪🌎🌿

Masked Trogons are common throughout the Andes cloud forests. These birds eat fruit and they also catch flying insects. This male was busy looking around for a meal.
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🪶🌿🧪

A Great Blue Heron from the Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska (last month). An everyday bird for many of you, well those in North America, but we are at the very edge of the range and lucky to get a sighting a year.

Pale-billed Flowerpecker seen on the IIT Bombay campus, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.

They're hard birds to get a good look at: Tiny (only 8 cm long), and they spend most of their time foraging for fruits and flowers (often from epiphytes and mistletoes) in the tree canopy.

🧪Meet the Steller's sea eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus), among the largest of all eagle species by both weight and wingspan! This individual is one half of a mated pair being cared for by Woodland Park Zoo as part of one of the U.S.’s few conservation breeding programs for this species 🦅
Abert’s towhee, foraging for goodies among the ground litter in Arizona. Spends most of its time on the ground, in fact. 🪶

American Kestrel

We found this pretty female murderbird along a roadside near Nanegalito, Ecuador. American Kestrels have a large range, from most of North American south into the western portion of South America and then throughout most of the southern portion of the continent.

A great part of Sayulita, Mexico: there's one patch of the Pacific Ocean that apparently is filled with the dumbest fish. Over and over brown pelicans speared into the water, shooting out their heads at the last moment like a snake striking to snatch fish from the water. #Mexico #Birds #Birding

The pyrrhuloxia or desert cardinal is a bird that inhabits the arid region from the southwestern United States to central Mexico.
It is similar to the northern cardinal in terms of song and behavior, and both coexist in many desert areas.

Since you’re enjoying the red-winged blackbird pic (& name), here are some tricolored blackbirds. Diff species. Found mostly in CA. Grassland specialists. Endangered.

Similar to red-winged, but males have white wing patch (not orange/yellow) and females are darker with clearer eyebrow stripe.

Kāruhiruhi fledglings on nest. Kāruhiru is the Māori name for the pied cormorant or shag. Photos taken at the Chelsea Heritage Park in Tāmaki-Makarau (Auckland). NZ has 12 of the 18 cormorant species, that exist globally.

Greater Yellowlegs and its little buddy Lesser Yellowlegs giving a nice rare comparison this evening at Steigerwald Lake in  Washougal, Washington. Greater is not interested in sharing its salamander. #birding

Barnegat part 11B: Harlequin Ducks edition continued. At Barnegat, these ducks like to hang out on the inlet side of the jetty, fairly close to the jetty walls. When they’ve shown up locally, they tend to hang out by our breakwalls along the beach, where Lake Erie tends to be the roughest. 🪶

Western meadowlark in front of a great egret over a vernal pool! 

Earlier this year, we had record rain in Southern California. Our local park has  vernal pools with fairy shrimp! 

An abundant water year made it possible for the scene to happen as western meadowlark’s winter in on our bluff￼ 🪶

Gang-gang Cockatoos! These endangered beauties are roughly the size of Galahs and limited to a small area of coastal South-Eastern Australia. The local flock visited our garden yesterday, and I counted *sixteen* of them!

Great egrets: masters of the art of striking a pose. With a slow, sassy strut, they wade through the waters in pursuit of their aquatic prey. Showcasing their prowess as ambush predators, these birds are striking in more than one way! Nature’s choreography unfolds in these feathered maestros.

I also heard weird noises coming from my chimney. It sounded like the crunching of bones.
I thought it was migratory birds at first, so two years ago I set up a sound-activated camera looking up the chimney to capture the source. It took this photo.

Bird of the day.. Waxwing (Bombycilla garrulus) We’ve had quite the irruption of these bandit masked bounty hunters this year! Our locale is blessed with juniper, rowan & mistletoe berries. They now move on to orchards new.. a joy all told these past few weeks..🪶

A Montane Woodcreeper working on a tasty moth. The bird is bashing the moth on the tree to knock off the scales on the moth's wings. These birds hitch up trees like woodpeckers while foraging for insects. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

A stunning Scarlet-Bellied Mountain Tanager working on some feather care - the bird was happily bathing. These fruit eating birds can be found in the high forests of the Andes Mountains from Venezuela to Bolivia. 🌿🧪🪶🐡
Zuroloma Birding, Nono, Ecuador
A great blue heron about to devour a crayfish.  Taken just a few days ago, 3-Oct-2023 in Sterling, Virginia, USA.

The resident Kingfisher at Rye Harbour Nature Reserve has been catching some rays of sunshine in the saltmarsh opposite the Discovery Centre. Amazing glimpses of metallic copper and cobalt blue.

This Broad-billed Motmot has evidence of its nest burrowing on its bill. It was busy digging until I rudely interrupted it this morning at Tapir Valley, Costa Rica.

When birding in your own garden in South Africa gets interesting. A pair of African Hoopoes successfully raised a Greater Honeyguide.

saw a bald eagle while clearing privet. as common as they are now it’s still a thrill.

Various waterbirds, taken from a great distance during our yurt stay last week: red-necked grebe, common loon, red-breasted mergansers. All perfectly at home on the aptly-named Bird Lake in Manitoba.

If you love birds, you need to conserve bugs. In this BugShot - Birding crossover event we have a Lemon-browed Flycatcher with a katydid. The bird say there with it so it was probably waiting for us to leave so it could head to its nest. 🌿🪶#inverts

It's about as far from the sea as you can get in the UK, and in the crowded south-east, but the diversity of birds around Oxford is just brilliant. Some of the highlights of the 167 species I recorded within 10km of home in 2023. No apologies for some distant record shots - it's birding!

A lone Kentish Plover flying with Sanderling. 
Since 2007, the breeding population in Spain of the KP has dropped by 52% to an estimated 3,300 pairs. Heavy tourism, beach cleaning, extreme temperatures & dogs off leads are some causes. 🪶
This half-ounce blackpoll warbler undertakes an epic migration journey - from the boreal forest north of here to spend winter in South America. It didn't stay long in my garden 🪶

One of the easiest, best things we've ever done for your yard is just deciding to let most of it be. We had a slim dead tree that fell towards the back last year, and this log has been a focal point for all kinds of wildlife, including this male Pileated Woodpecker this morning. 🪶
iconic Australian landscape - superb parrots at their nest hollow in an old red gum, with kangaroos grazing in the shade

Good to see the resident pied oystercatchers at Bald Hill Beach working on their family planning. They have been so unlucky. Every year they try. Every year they fail to become parents. Maybe this time?
