The wiki entry for Say's phoebe says, "it prefers dry, desolate areas." So, like, a very popular beach. 😁

Natural Bridges SB, Santa Cruz, CA

Sunday afternoon went birding with a friend in South Louisiana. As the sun was setting we noticed a large amount of Scissor-tailed Flycatchers. A careful 1x1 count and got us 53! As we were leaving they started to roost in the Oak trees. Quite a sight just an hour south of New Orleans!
Warbler migration is ramping up. Fall warblers can be hard to identify, but thankfully black-and-white warblers are always recognizable. 🪶

One of my favorite local birding patches is a park with a mowed lawn, a few gigantic trees, and a shrub-lined creek

It's a great migrant trap, those shrubs get loaded with warblers

Today, though, there was also a gorgeous Barred Owl in the lawn trees, my first there! (photo taken from a distance)

Our Halloween-colored #BirdGlamour celebration continues with the Hooded Oriole! Seen in southwest US and Mexico, they’re also known as palm leaf orioles because the sew their sack nests on the undersides of palm fronds. Hooded Orioles are chatterboxes and will mimic woodpeckers and flycatchers!🧪🐡🐦
Why is one of these rocks chewing on a piece of horsetail?  Collared Pika watching the world go by from a rock-field near Anchorage #Alaska 🦊

In one of my audiobook groups someone asked for “weirdest/grossest/most disturbing” horror novels. Some people are suggesting Dean Koontz and James Patterson. I mean, I guess experiences may vary. I just sent them a link to @@62103’s Splattepunk awards.
If you are a space person, you are going to want to read Samantha Harvey's ORBITAL, a remarkable evocation of life on the International Space Station. Stunning, beautiful, profound.

finished reading 'Saint Of Bright Doors' by Vajra Chandrasekera. 

You need to read this. One of the best books I've read this year. Chandrasekera created an incredibly messy and meaty world full of terror and religious wonder, with beautiful prose that doesn't let up for the entire book.

No Shelter But The Stars by Virginia Black, for a story that primarily takes place on a barren rock or in space, this novel is so viscerally lush with emotional depth! Even the most spartan passages hit so that you feel it with your whole chest. Just a hugely satisfying read.

Sapphic books | WLW

Piranesi was powerful enough to haunt me with dreams of stone halls, with sea and statues and mysterious messages, their chalk half-wiped away. 

Immediately one of my favourite books ever. I can still hear and feel it.

The Saint of Bright Doors reads like a novel-length short story in the best way possible: full of fresh ideas, fast-moving, with an evident care applied to each and every sentence. Remarkable and highly-recommended.

Weekly book talk for Saturday, February 4. What’s everyone reading?

If I had the power to compel 330 million Americans to read one and only one book, it would be The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965).

Let's talk pretty writing in fantasy. I'm thinking of books like Erin Morgenstern's The Starless Sea, The Ten Thousand Doors of January by Alix E Harrow, The Necessity of Rain by Sarah Chorn, and Piranesi by Susanna Clarke. Who would you add? Share your favorites!

Finished Solenoid by Mircea Cartarescu, tr Sean Cotter. A battle at times, but worth the effort I think. A richly imaginative stream/waterslide of consciousness slipping between dream states and reality for a nobody Romanian school teacher. Lots of great lines and tough paragraphs.

I am very happy to learn the French edition of my 2016 novel REVOLVER won this year's Rivages Booksellers' Prize, which is chosen by a jury of 96 booksellers in France, Chile, Morocco and Quebec! A high-five to my brilliant translator, Sophie Aslanides, for her amazing work.

Finished Farthings by Charles Boyle – 113 short pieces (a para each) about life as a one man publishing house CBEditions. I enjoyed it enormously. Like Alan Bennett's diaries only with a sharper edge. Miraculously free of industry bitterness, but lots of wry insights about how publishing works.

I have finished reading the latest @@4966 book, What Feasts at Night, and can tell you that the author is one of the best writers of the experience of having a nightmare that I have ever read. Just, captures the fuckin' WEIRDNESS of bad dreams.

A++ 
10/10
No notes.

I woke up early and couldn’t get back to sleep, so read the brilliant Sexcastle by @@144959 

It’s a riot from start to finish, about an assassin trying to live a peaceful life, until…

If you dig classic action movies, John Wick, Road House etc. it’s a must!

He Who Drowned The World by @@166678 is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

Announcing the winner of the 2023 Cercador Prize: @@241041’s translation of Ana Paula Maia's OF CATTLE AND MEN.

The Cercador committee says OF CATTLE AND MEN "lays bare the brutal labor of the slaughterhouse trade," hailing the novel as a "new breed of western."

Incredible little work, this - like Ford’s WELL-BUILT CITY by way of WE HAVE ALWAYS LIVED IN THE CASTLE. One of the most effectively moving finales I’ve ever read. Remarkably consonant with MOTHTOWN. What a time to be alive and reading books about death.

I’ve been reading Discworld for the first time this year and I cannot overstate how good it is. It’s funny, smart, and has just layers of “oh that’s a good point…” that makes you think and hopefully grow.

I wish I had read them sooner.

Absolutely blown away by C. J. Leede's MAEVE FLY, which sets itself higher and higher hurdles and then backflips over every one of them. It invites so many comparisons veteran authors would quake at, but it earns them. Mean, nasty, solipsistic, amazing.

The Case of Cem by Bulgarian writer Vera Mutafchieva deserves to be part of the 20th century canon of global literature. Now, thanks to International Booker winner Angela Rodel, Anglophone readers can discover this epic novel. These are finished copies. Let me know if you want an ARC.

Just finished Malcolm Lowry’s first novel, Ultramarine. A bit purple, and fascinating regarding how much a writer insists on getting out of the “will he cheat on his lady back home question” and whether or not he will get in a fight with the ship’s resident tough dude. Not a bad intro to Lowry.
if you like heists and crooks who aren’t very good at being crooks, i’m begging you to read donald e. westlake’s Dortmunder novels

Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship & personal breakdown. Simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I have ever read. One of the best too. I'm used to book sadness, book poignancy, book emotions – but this is the real thing.

Today's book rec is Paul Beatty's THE SELLOUT, which possesses in great measure what all powerful satires possess: beneath its comedic madcap zaniness there is a scratching, a tearing; an overwhelming sense of the terror of history bubbling up from below; a profound and wholly justified cosmic rage.

Just finished my 10th book of the year with a re-read of The Blade Itself, which is still as incredible as it was when I first read it 18 (🤮) years ago. Really looking forward to revisiting the series, and then reading A Little Hatred and the sequels for the first time.

Happy New Year! Starting Pedro Páramo by Juan Rulfo, translated from the Spanish by Douglas J. Weatherford. First book of the year. For newly formed novella book club. And because Sontag called it “one of the masterpieces of twentieth-century world literature.”

Happy #PubDay to this mighty, mighty book. 

In 1997, Sarabande published LAST CALL, a poetry anthology that became a formative text on the lived experiences of addiction. Now, more than 25 years later, editors Kaveh Akbar and Paige Lewis offer this companion volume for a new generation.

Am reading The Little Rebel, by French author Jérôme Leroy and it is amazing. The story of tensions and terror in a French city, it’s immensely powerful and thought-provoking. It’s only 80 pages and we’ll worth a read.

PROMISE is another SUPERB batch of strange, eerie, unearthly stories from @@4103588. Weird futures, fantastical technologies, and unusual histories populate these worlds where Reality shimmers like a mirage of a dream: it may seem similar to ours, but it's never quite the same.

This is a terrific novel. Doherty was from Scotland but worked as a miner near Rotherham - he's really important in that tradition of post-war working-class Yorkshire fiction that leads to Stan Barstow, John Braine, David Storey and the rest.

Semi-recently read Poe's PYM and Mat Johnson's PYM back to back and I highly recommend this, the experience is absolutely hilarious. Book 1 of 2024 is done. Dexter & Sinister Detecting Agents by Keith W. Dickinson.

A fun steampunk murder mystery with a talking cat. Can you ask for more? Well, okay, Dexter is not a 'real' cat, as he is quick to point out, but that just makes him more sarcastic.

Climate change is deeply unfair in terms of who are responsible for it and who suffer the most from it.

The world’s richest 1% are responsible for 15% of global CO₂ emissions.

In contrast, the bottom 50% are responsible for only 8% of CO₂ emissions.

Companies that seek a future in the 21st century cannot ignore risks from extreme weather events made worse by climate change or big economy-wide impacts that will come from trying to mitigate climate change and adapt to warming.
Vicious cycle: Global warming leads to beavers 🦫 moving north, where beaver ponds thaw the permafrost and release methane, leading to greater warming.

European Rechnungshof gave EU's CO₂ fleet regulation scathing marks: By 2020, "the intended benefits of the regulation had largely lapsed". The Commission had given manufacturers the wrong incentives, plus: has not led to the desired reduction in CO₂ emissions from newly registered vehicles.

The latest COP28 climate negotiations ended with the nations of the world – for the first time ever – agreeing to transition away from fossil fuels that are driving the climate crisis.

Even if CO₂ emissions decreased annually as much as during the pandemic lockdown, we wouldn't achieve our climate targets.

While individuals made huge sacrifices, the system remained. This shows that individual action will not solve climate change. We need the system to change.

November 17, 2023 was the first day in recorded history when the earth's surface was a whole 2°C hotter than pre-industrial times.

Temperatures have to surpass the 2°C benchmark for months and years at a time before scientists consider it breached, but we have moved into uncharted territory.

For the first time since August daily global surface temperatures are back down below record levels. 

This is not particularly surprising, as we tend to see larger variability in daily global temps in winter months. We are still on track for December to be the warmest on record.

4. Use a chicken brick. Roemertopf. Clay pot.

Today's Gardening Advice: Check seed packet date ranges prior to starting seedlings.  Some vegetable seeds germinate quickly and grow just as fast.  Too often I hear from people, "Oh, I started my tomatoes in January and now they're all leggy." Yes, wait, read directions, don't start too soon. 🌱

Tired: Harvard Extension School isn't a prestigious program, it's basically the same education you'd get at the local community college

An amazing thing about academia is that it simultaneously demands you do world-changing, revolutionary work and then does everything it can to prevent you from actually doing that work.

West Virginia's conservative government flushing WVU down the drain for no real reason is a good reminder that the causation arrow of "economically backwards place develops reactionary politics" is often the opposite of how people assume it is.
Always find the, "so-and-so university has been ranked best in the world" coverage funny, because...best at what? It sure as heck is never diversity, accessibility, or wellbeing.

Conservatives are aiming to wreck higher ed, but they’re going to have to work pretty hard to outdo the damage that higher ed inflicted on itself by training ten times as many academics as there are academic positions.

One of the most frustrating things about academia is the longer I’m in it, the more I know via direct experience or friends experiences about how many academics who present themselves as the kindest, most inclusive mentors are the biggest bullies to junior colleagues.

One of the sad problems w/ being the only black student in majority rich, white spaces of higher academia are the feelings of loneliness, casual racism, microaggressions & general othering that you get either on purpose or by accident from both students & even worse, teachers alike. It's alienating.
The number 1 mistake of educational program solutions is the idea that education is purely a set of ideas and facts rather than, at base, a form of teacher-student relationship.

If you are on a committee that's a mix of faculty and staff, and faculty keep suggesting lofty ideas while staff members are the only ones doing the actual labor and follow-through, you should definitely name and disrupt that dynamic

i knew the academic job market is tricky but I don’t think I had fully appreciated the role of timing. You may be someone who would be great at being a professor but only a handful of positions that are a good fit for you may become available during your postdoc years

It was always obvious that the Conservative government’s concern wasn’t really about academic freedom or free speech on campus, but about protecting certain kinds of speech from certain academics, while creating the conditions for further government intervention in higher education.

I know a lot of people are just working off of templates, but I think it's always worth thinking about whether the job listing you are creating actually requires a college education and why you think it does because I would bet that very few of them actually do.

The biggest difference I've noticed between Academia and Industry is in academia everyone wants to be in all the meetings because they are SO important and obvs the meeting won't be as good without them. Industry is about avoiding them as much as possible, we have actual shit to do.

I know we all have signed on to suffer together under this collective delusion that having gone to Harvard says something about your intelligence, but as someone who waited on the students and professors for 15 years I promise you they are all just some fucking nerd who can barely navigate the world

This summer I grew alpine strawberries for the first time. Not only did they taste much more delicious than my regular strawberries, they were easier to grow from seed and produced constantly from spring to the last frost. I got a far bigger harvest from these than from my regular ones. 🌱🍓

“They say university leaders bumbled into catastrophe by spending millions of dollars on top-end salaries, athletics, risky expansion efforts and tuition subsidies for out-of-state students. Now they worry that middle-class workers and Arizona students will be hit hardest by efforts to cut budgets.”

Haunted Mansion is absolutely everything I could have wanted given that it wasn’t straight horror. It’s a great gateway horror movie that pays a lot of respect to the lore of the attraction and its creators while addressing grief and loss. I loved it so much!

Love Lies Bleeding was everything I hoped it would be. One of the absolute best modern sapphic films, equally hot, passionate, toxic and uncomfortable. It's complex and messy and never shies away from taking the most visceral punch possible. Absolutely loved it, best film I've seen this year.

feeling brave

best concert I’ve ever been to was Simon & Garfunkel. show itself wasn’t a spectacle. but the most pristine vocals I’ve ever experienced. they sounded BETTER in person than any recording

I'm not a musicologist, but I feel pretty certain that there is no song that can't be made better by the drummer raising the drumsticks over their head and slamming them together four times before the song starts.

Nora just squirted her kitty anal gland secretions all over my keyboard and my hand. If you've ever had a cat do this, it is the most foul-smelling thing you've ever experienced. I'm going to chop my hand off now, which is not a thing I needed on the most important work deadline of the year.
The cats are like “HOLY SHIT you’re MOVING THINGS AROUND that means they’re ALL NEW THINGS”. Cats are the avatars of adhd

Most cats in the UK have the opportunity to stay away from fast-moving vehicles. Seems like it's one of the safer places, generally. Especially since here in the North of England black cats are considered GOOD luck (although we do keep them in on Halloween because of twats).

all night every night our livestock guardian dog wakes up every couple of hours to stroll over to the other half of the house and go back to sleep

90% of the time it's cute when he just Goes On Patrol like that

10% of the time he perceives literally anything unusual and calls an emergency meeting

I have misophonia and any kind of eating sound will make me have walmart line thoughts about you-- unless you're a cat.
Then something happens. I don't know. The ovaries put their foot down.

I really like how happy the Orioles logo is. 99% of sports animal logos are snarling and angry and vicious, like they're trying to strike fear into people. But this oriole? He's just loving life, happy to be here. It's refreshing.

If you were to make a list of the absolute worst things a powerful leader can do, genocide and carelessness with nuclear brinkmanship should be on the very top of that list. 

Biden has done both of these.

You can’t legitimately call such a person a “lesser evil”.

This has to be said because too many Democrats and nonvoters are missing it: there were 70 million Americans who found they preferred being citizens subject to authoritarianism to being citizens in a democracy. They didn't go away because Biden squeaked by. They won't be seduced, they must be beaten

One of the toughest parts of being a major league batter is not only do you have to hit some impossible 98-mph sinker that isn't even detectable by a normal human eye, but after you strike out the umpire does a huge fist pump

Since we all fucking insist on having “yellow ladders in video games” discourse again I’d like to reiterate that it’s an unfortunate side effect of the inscrutable need to make game environments so realistic that you have to make anything you can do video game things to dayglo so people can play.

My hot take after The Game Awards is developers' work was pretty poorly celebrated tonight (as evidenced by the "wrap it up after 30 seconds" speeches and multiple awards hammered out in a row). The "Awards" part is grossly underrepresented, and most gamers sadly couldn't care less about that.

Pretty ridiculous for The Game Awards (which I recognize are themselves ridiculous) to give an award for “Most Anticipated Game.” I honestly thought our industry had already learned the lesson about giving awards to games that aren’t actually out yet.
gamers need to be able to sit with the fact that not every game is for every person and their own desires for features or fixes are not failures on the game. play something else, dude
St. Andrew, Slaidburn in Lancashire is notable for the high quality of its woodwork. The chancel screen dates from the 1630's and is highly decorated.

Quite a thrill this week to see the ancient ruins at Akrotiri, on the island of Santorini. Remains of a once thriving and wealthy civilisation, destroyed when most of the island exploded in a volcanic eruption around 1600 BC. 

#AncientSiteSunday

ReliefWednesday; from the Museo Nacional de Arte Romano in Mérida, a banqueting scene related to Mithras, whose rock birth is depicted in a sculpture at the left of the scene. Found locally in Mérida at the Casa dei Mitreo.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Very sexy folds developed in thinly interbedded limestones and shales of the Cretaceous Palombini shale formation at Acquarilli beach, Island of Elba.

This is one of the formations that was squeezed in an accretionary prism before the collision that generated the Northern Apennines.

The trireme, a marvel of military ship building. Here are 2 ancient reliefs, the 1st from Rhodes carved into a cliff face (📷Saffron Blaze), the 2nd from Athens (📷Acropolis 🏛️). The other 2 pics are the ‘Olympias’, a modern reconstruction which an inexperienced crew got to 17kph! 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

I love research news about neanderthals, and this one is exciting; "This fundamentally changes our previous knowledge about the period: Homo sapiens reached northwestern Europe long before Neanderthal disappearance in southwestern Europe.": phys.org/news/2024-01... 🧪
Smyths Almshouses, Maidenhead, 1659; built and endowed by James Smyth, born locally, later an alderman of the City of London and a member of the Worshipful Company of Salters.
Fireworks frozen in space. First sight of the vaulting at Christ Church Cathedral in Oxford draws the breath. It’s hard to believe that such a fluid expression in stone was built around 1400.

Bigger than life-size 1st century reproduction of the statue of Athene Promachos which stood on the Acropolis. Original by Pheidias c.456 BC. 

This marble statue was discovered in the Villa dei Papiri, Herculaneum. 

🏛 National Archaeological Museum Naples 

🏺Ancientbluesky

Bramante’s Tempietto, door from inside.
Donato Bramante, Tempietto, c. 1502, San Pietro in Montorio, Rome. The “Tempietto” or little temple is a martyia (a building that commemorates a martyrdom) that marks the traditional site of Saint Peter’s crucifixion.

St. Patrick's, Patrington, Yorks: "Flying buttresses scarcely suffice to tether the needle-sharp...most tapering of all these spires to its tower, and the designer has invented a ravishing cusped and pinnacled corona of arcading to steady it." Olive Cook

A stunning Roman bowl made of vivid blue glass. It was found in Nijmegen (Netherlands) in absolutely perfect condition - without even the slightest fracture or chip - despite being around 2000 years ago. An absolutely amazing find! 🏺AncientBlueSky

For ClassicsTober day 3; part of a statue depicting Asterion, the Minotaur. A 1st century CE Roman copy of a group with Theseus dating to the 5th century BCE, possibly sculpted by Myron. Found in Rome and now in the Museo Nazionale Romano, Palazzo Massimo alle Terme.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

The Genoese built a series of towers and walls to fortify their colony at Galata (across the Golden Horn from Constantinople) in the 14th and 15th century. In addition to Galata Tower, much more survives than is commonly known.

Stèle de Saint-Patrice monolith in the wall of a private garden, Lannion, Côtes-D'Armor. A large Christianized Gaulish stele which once held several statues of saints. Also called "menhir of justice", having probably served as a gallows. 🏺 More 1/

Stonehenge origin mystery solved as experts find evidence megalith 'first stood in Wales'

Extensive research found a dismantled stone circle in West Wales that was moved to the current site of Stonehenge thousands of years ago.

The world's oldest condom, made of pig intestines in 1640 CE, discovered in Lund, Sweden. The owners manual advised men to dip it in unboiled milk before use to prevent diseases when engaging with prostitutes. It’s washable and reusable. On display at Tirolean County Museum in Austria.

#FrescoFriday #ClassicsTober23 27: Pygmalion

This fresco depicts Polyphemus and the nereid Galatea in a landscape. Galatea is sometimes also the name of the statue that Pygmalion creates, though these were likely different story traditions…

🏺ClassicsBluesky

Two marble herms from the so-called Greek Theatre at Hadrian's Villa representing Comedy and Tragedy. Today in the Vatican Museums in the Sala Rotonda of the Pio Clementino Museum.
followinghadrian.com/2017/03/21/a...
Engraved Giraffes and Cow, around 8000 years old (Neolithic Period), at Wadi Mathendous prehistoric archaeological site - Messak, Settafet, Libya.

A paper in Nature reports on artefacts from an archaeological site in Ukraine estimated to be around 1.4 million years old, which represent the earliest securely dated evidence for hominin presence in Europe. go.nature.com/4388Uk3 🧪🏺

Agrippina the Elder, mother of Caligula. 
Born in Athens, she hailed from the Julio-Claudian dynasty. 
She dressed her son in traditional soldier’s attire including the caliga from which he would derive his name and be known to the annals of history: Caligula.
🏛 Capitoline Museums
🏺Ancientbluesky

The Colosseum in Rome completed using drones to light it. 😲 It was done by a Dutch artist called Duo at DRIFT Studios in Amsterdam, using sophistacted software & drones. He's also done other iconic structures that have passed the test of time... its quite impressive.

news.artnet.com/news/see-how...

Up to Derry for tonight's Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺

Derry has a fantastic variety of monuments, from one of the earliest with the Mesolithic site at Mountsandel to the walled city. Here's some of my favourites:

1. Standing stones at Clagan
2. The King's Fort
3. Derry City Walls.

As you may know, I love old photographs of historical sites and this is a corker. It’s the Pantheon, mid-19th century and was taken by the Fratelli d’Allesandri studio. Looks very similar to today except perhaps for the pony and trap. 

🏺 AncientBlueSky

No better way to end my year of church crawling with the Jesse window from St Mary the Virgin, Shrewsbury, Shropshire. 

This C14 window was moved twice - was originally at the Greyfriars chapel along the Severn. How it survived the Reformation is unclear.

A beauty. 

🗃️#medievalsky

Scarab bracelet of lapis lazuli set in gold, from previously-unlooted 3,300-year-old tomb of Pharaoh Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings, excavated by Howard Carter in 1922. Scarabs (dung beetles) were associated with eternal cycle of life theancientwayoflife.tumblr.com/post/1702036...

Boxed in.
Simple yet effective mosaic threshold with geometric design dating to the 1st century BC from the Roman Villa of Fannius Synistor in the Vesuvian area (Boscoreale). Currently on display in the Antiquarium at Boscoreale.

Hello, bluesky! I often share photos of beautiful things using the #SomethingBeautiful tag, usually things from Italy (where I do research). This is a wrought iron dragon bracket on the side of a Medieval building in Assisi. If you'd enjoy photos of history & beauty, you'll find them here!

The Temple of the Nymphs in Rome was founded in the 3rd century BCE. 

The ruins seen here were discovered on the Via delle Botteghe Oscure during building work in 1938 and the columns were re-erected in 1954.

If ever there was a night to show the beauty humanity can make, it's this.
This is the Torr chamfrein, from W. Scotland , developed/late Iron Age, where the magic happens. At least part of it was a decorative cap for a pony, tho its biog is a lot more complex.

Straight from the earth somewhere in Suffolk, a beautifully decorated Beaker Period Bronze Axe. 4000 years old, in almost mint condition and of obvious museum quality.

This is the simply MAGNIFICENT early 13th century imperial crown of Constance of Aragon, who was wife of the Holy Roman Emperor, Frederick II, known as stupor mundi (or, wonder of the world). It’s housed in the Treasury of Palermo Cathedral.

The worst bird pic is the one that is almost a best bird pic.

Blurry pyrrhuloxia at the Bosque del Apache National Wildlife Refuge.

🪶🦉Almost as beautiful,  the little egret isn't really that much smaller than yesterday's great egret. Especially if you keep in mind the cattle egret, which is chicken-sized. Getting more common in Belgium every year. Lovely. (Kleine zilverreiger, Egretta garzetta)

Blue-winged Mountain Tanagers are common, widespread tanagers found in the Andean cloud forest. Their favorites food are insects, fruits, and small berries. And yes, this bird is a stunner. It is unmistakable with its bright yellow, black, and electric blue coloring. 🪶

Hundreds of canvasback ducks in the Niagara River yesterday. Was able to count four separate paddlings but nowhere to pull over along the Niagara Parkway for closer pics. Here are two of the four mass gatherings we came across, always an amazing sight to see even on a very dull (& difficult) day. 🪶

Most woodpeckers are black and white. This is true in South America too, but the Crimson Mantled Woodpecker breaks that trend. They are considered common, but this summer was the first time I saw one. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Black Phoebes used to be rare to infrequent in Oregon, especially the Northern parts, but now are more and more common. This one graced our presence at Eagle Lookout at Ankeny National Wildlife Refuge today in Marion County, Oregon. #birding

Parenting of swallows. The swallow's parent bird flies at great speed and shoves a large dragonfly into the chick's mouth. The chicks also try their best to drink the dragonfly. It's powerful.
#birds🪶
A male Golden-browed Chlorophonia (Chlorophonia callophrys) enjoying fresh berries for breakfast at Paraiso Quetzal Lodge, Costa Rica.

Quite rare around Anchorage #Alaska, I was surprised to meet the Double-crested Cormorant while kayaking earlier this fall. I guess it was surprised to see me too as it declined to stay on the same lake as me.

Mt Laguna is also home to one of my all-time favorite birds: the Acorn Woodpecker! This time they were busy gorging themselves on the abundant insects flying over Little Laguna Lake, unseasonably filled by the tropical storm 🪶

The mandarin duck (Aix galericulata) is considered one of the most beautiful birds in the world.
      It is a native of China and Japan.The first mandarins were imported to Britain in the mid-18th century, but it wasn't until the 1930s that escapes from wildfowl collections started breeding here.

Swallow-tailed Cotinga in the Serra do Mar outside Rio de Janeiro last month - a #bird I hadn't expected to see as bad weather forced me to cancel a planned day of higher altitude birding, so a wonderful surprise when this one turned outside the lodge as I waited out rainstorms. 🪶 #BirdsOfBrazil

It has a pinkish-brown body, striking black and white wings, a black downcurved bill, and a pinkish-brown crest which it raises when excited.
   The Eurasian hoopoe is native to Europe, Asia and the northern half of Africa.

"In a spectacular sight, flocks of various species of water birds migrating from different parts of Eurasia have started arriving at Point Calimere Wildlife and Bird Sanctuary in Nagapattinam district."
Check out these beauties. Often overlooked, stock doves are the classy, understated cousins of the clumsy, oafish wood pigeon. 🪶
🦉🪶 From afar, the lapwing looks like a black-and-white bird with a flying disorder, but up close, it is far more colorful.  Still unable to fly straight, tho. (kievit, Vanellus vanellus)

A MacGillivray’s warbler unexpectedly hopping around in the Bronx. A vagrant species usually found west of the Rockies, it was found by a 13 year old on their local Christmas bird count! #birds #birdsnyc 🪶

Outside of Kazakhstan: Finally we could watch the migration of the Eurasian crane in Kelbra, Germany. It was fantastic to see thousands of birds arriving in dusk at this lake to fly later further to Spain. These birds also living in Kazakhstan and migrating in winter to India.

Thought my walk at my local birding patch this morning was going to consist of chilling with some wrens and sparrows and then found a LEWIS'S FREAKING WOODPECKER, right here in town. Migration is wild, y'all. 🪶

🧪One of the coolest critters I’ve ever seen, an aptly named rhinoceros katydid (Copiphora rhinoceros) spotted in the Monteverde area of Costa Rica. I’ve never been much of an entomology gal, but this guy sold me 🦗

The kingfisher that lives by my place finally agreed to let me photograph her! Just after this she caught a fish nearly a third her size and proceeded to whack it against the branch until it was dead.

Belted Kingfisher - San Francisco, CA 🪶

Parked next to a field yesterday. Not much to see but then a red-tailed hawk came and landed on a bush directly in front of me. 

That apparently displeased the neighborhood kestrel which kept buzzing the rather nonplussed hawk.

Yesterday while out with the chickens as they free ranged, we saw the world's tiniest hawk regarding us from a tree branch above—presumably a male sharp-shinned hawk. He was about the size of a pigeon.

BARN SWALLOW ATTACK! 🪶

Record year of rain meant we got vernal pools on the bluff! 

The buffet of bugs attracted the largest mixed flock of swallows I’ve ever seen at our park

It gave many opportunities to miss focus on swallows. And a few lucky hits!

god i love watching a reddish egret just launch itself through the water, wings out, head swinging to and fro. It's a hilarious method of hunting AND IT WORKS. these dudes rustle up a ton of food running around like a maniac in the water.

Some underwater critters from Saturday out at Tāwharanui. You'll see spotties, snapper, goat fish, sweep, hiwihiwi and a few eagle rays, one feeding. Also, a lot of attempts to capture the beautiful colours of the seaweed, the ground and the sponges. #MarineLife 🦑 #Kikorangi #Aotearoa

I hear it's Black-crowned night heron day. Count me in. They're such sleek birds. Found on every continent except Australia and Antarctica, they are the most widespread heron species in the world! #Birds
More from the backyard bird habitat...here's a very tiny pair of African silverbills (Lonchura cantans) coming for their daily adorable drink and bath. Wee seed eaters really like our yard. :) 🦉📷🌿

The cunning and mischievous cockatoos visited to eat berries, chew up the house and do squawking photobombs. Last pic shows one eating one of my grandson's crusts - not suitable bird food, even if they like it.
#birds

In good light, the range of colours on rainbow lorikeets 🪶 seems to be infinite. They really wear the whole visible spectrum. #ausbirds
Dowse Lagoon, Brisbane QLD

we've had a lot of activity from one of my favorite birds, the black-footed albatross, who mainly nests along the leeward islands of Hawai'i & forages hundreds of miles of the North Pacific for food to feed their chicks

🌊🦑🪶🌿📷🌎

This kestrel was hovering low on a breezy day at Cuckmere. It was amazing to see how it could keep its head so perfectly still whilst being buffeted by the wind.

#birds #ukwildlife

Barnegat part 10…A😅: Long-tailed Ducks. One of two duck species this spot is particularly famous for. There were several huge rafts feeding well beyond the inlet of hundreds of ducks, but plenty up close, too. Took tons of pics, so first up are the males in their fancy winter colors. 🪶

Cedar Waxwing
 
They are elegant-looking birds named for beads of shiny red material on the tips of the secondary wing feathers.
      It breeds in northern forests of Eurasia and America and every few years irrupts far southward in winter.
An Anna's hummingbird (Calypte anna) showing off that stunning iridescent gorget, aka the lil patch of feathers on its neck. Photographed in Seattle, WA 🧪🐡
A mallard drake's plumage catches the light four different ways. They're so common that we forget about how fabulous they are somtimes #birds
Winter has come to Portland and the birds are doing their best to keep warm. This Hermit Thrush wears a puffed mantle of feathers while visiting Our Lady of Guadalupe. #birding

Spotted this handsome Limpkin at City Park in New Orleans. In Nashville, we have a lone Limpkin at Centennial Park that caused quite a stir, being uncommon to the area. He's still hanging out there despite the fact that the cold has set in. The snails must be delectable. #birding #birdwatching
These are black-and-gold howler monkeys (Alouatta caraya), and live in South America. The have sexually dimorphic colors—males are black, females are gold! 🧪
#birds here is a yellow rumped  warbler on a red maple, these guys are all over Southeast Louisiana right now munching on wax myrtle berries, which is why they used to be called myrtle warblers.

This is the best book about space exploration I've read in a very long time, and if you, too, dream of exploring space without exploiting it, then you, too, will end up with your copy festooned with Post-it notes. physicsworld.com/a/towering-e...

I'm currently reading an early proof of The Silverblood Promise, the debut novel by James Logan (JFB, April 2024). Oh man, if you liked The Lies of Locke Lamora you're going to *love* this. 

The pyramid game is one of the most sadistic things I think I've ever read; worthy of Hellraiser, almost.
whoever was looking for book recommendations, "canticle for liebowitz" is the best book I have ever read, a position it has held since I first encountered it almost four decades ago

I love paying honor to my Blackness through jokes, stories and the oral tradition.

That said, this a book that I *always* read. It's a compendium of Black folklore going all the way back to Africa. It's always relevant, thought-provoking, and it's the funniest damn book in the world.

Just finished Lucifer by Dan Watters, Max Fiumara, Sebastian Fiumara et al. 

Couldn’t hit my sweet spot more if it tried. Gorgeous art, great story and definitely a strong compliment to the wider Sandman world.

finally read this essay and it's really good. But in mirroring my own memories of this time, a lot of the New Weird was heavily dude-coded. Indeed my favorite novel of that milieu The Etched City by KJ Bishop was never mentioned! (You should read it) textjournal.scholasticahq.com/article/1856...
Highly, highly recommend her book Signal to Noise as well. It’s about kids in 1980s Mexico City casting spells with mixtapes.

Wonderful Sunday reading: this Mexican novel, a fragmentary non-chronological Bildungsroman based on Yukio Mishima’s travels to Mexico and the US as well as his formation as a writer. One of the best Mexican books I’ve read recently. I hope it makes it to translation

I have just finished Bombshell by Sarah MacLean, and it is--to me-- the platonic ideal of a romance novel. Banter! Smexy! A thick thighed hero who's got big Roy Kent vibes! A heroine who's proficient in explosions! I giggled REGULARLY. 

10/5 stars, highest rec.

A BULLET IS WAITING is much more a romantic melodrama thriller than anything hardboiled (heck, there’s a lil lamb!), but as someone who grew up in the Rockies & the southwest, I’ll always be a sucker for something set in this part of the country (Utah, here.) A colorful, comfy #Noirvember pick.

Books of 2023. One of the two or three best debuts of 2023, Some Desperate Glory takes the age old stuff of space opera and makes it fresh and new. Be sure to read Abigail Nussbaum's review.

publishing.tor.com/somedesperat...

Incidents Around The House marks the evolution of Josh Malerman. Adept prose and commanding storytelling that effortlessly draws the reader in for a deeply unsettling and genuinely frightening read. A masterclass in scares. Highly recommended!

Still buzzing at how much I enjoyed 'Anyone But You'. An absolutely terrific romcom. Also shows how basically if you just cleave to the principles of a Nora Ephron romance you'll probably make a great romcom. And if you cleave to those principles while riffing off Shakespeare, you're cooking.

I am finally reading the Murderbot books after almost everyone I have ever met telling me to do so. And, one book down, they DO NOT disappoint. It reminds me of various 2000Ad strips, in a very good way.

I just finished Mariana Enriquez's Things We Lost in the Fire and damn! I don't know if I've ever read a short story collection with more affecting pacing. The stories are staggered so you never know how far the Horror will creep next time.

i dont read scp wiki as religiously as in past; but this story is incredible- a perfect skewer, unsettling, horrifying in ways that 'scary murder entity' cant get close to

Finished THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS by @@64002; what a tremendous reading experience. Do NOT let this one fade from your sight, friends. 

Buy a copy, and experience a story that was by turns tender & Weird, surprising & horrific, beautiful in all angles. WHEW. I gotta lie down.

Few & far between are the times when I feel my whole body mantling towards the vicious rage of a petty tyrant but one of the things that reliably gets me there is hitting the precise point in a seemingly standalone novel where it becomes clear it’s Book 1 of a series

I just finished N.K. Jemisin's The City We Became, and it's gorgeous. 

My favorite author is William Gibson, and I'm not really drawing a comparison in the writing. But there was similarity in the electric *feeling* of reading the book. I'm so happy.

Book 1 of 2024 is Different Seasons by Stephen King 

I had never read any Stephen King before this and I was simply blown away. Four very different stories that affected me in so many different ways. Reading him is like when a scissors starts to glide through wrapping paper. Effortless.

Amal El-Mohtar is wonderfully generous to THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS in the New York Times: “the best book I’ve read all year. Protean, singular, original … I can’t remember the last time a book made me so excited about its existence, its casual challenge to what a fantasy novel could be.”

Really enjoying Witches Abroad so far. Love how the witches play off of each other, and also love what the story does with fairy tales. Actually wonder if Shrek took any inspiration from it. Also very much feels like Pterry visited New Orleans once and was like. Ah yes. I know this place. Uh. GUMBO

The latest from the British Library’s Tales of the Weird series has arrived: Circles of Stone - Weird Tales of Pagan Sites and Ancient Rites. The stories include Machen, Blackwood and less well known authors. Also Quatermass’ Nigel Kneale whom I wrote about recently.

#pbarb2023books Songs of a Dead Dreamer and Grimscribe by Thomas Ligotti. My first Ligotti book-length work & it’s been well-loved. Enjoyed it, especially the slow burn unsettling of reality carried across the stories.

Black Mischief is one of those ‘secondary’ Waugh novels—like Put Out More Flags—that I’ve never felt the urge to reread. But here Will Lloyd makes the case for it as the novel where “Waugh discovers that he is serious.”

A charming book about grieving for lost time, and moving from childhood into teenage-hood. The main girls’ friendship is endearing & warm like a soft blanket. In true Reimena Yee fashion, the art is whimsical and breaks panel borders in engaging ways!

"SILENT KEY is one hell of a read. In this masterful melding of multiple genres, Hightower weaves mystery and espionage into a stellar twist on the ghost story. This book deserves to make it into every airport bookshop and supermarket spinner rack. Laurel Hightower is one to watch!"

One of the books that folks brought up a lot when @@89192 asked about the most intimidating book on their TBR was Arno Schmidt’s Bottom’s Dream, miraculously translated by John E. Woods. There is good reason for the intimidation. Here is page 1 of 1,493!
finished reading "Crossings" by Ben Goldfarb today, I really enjoyed it!  absolutely fascinating science, and a gorgeous & compelling writing style.
Just finished @@22511 WISH I WAS HERE, a slim volume with surprising lateral kick and a punk attitude tossing out any expectation of linear architecture in the memoir genre. Hard rec!

If you are having a high anxiety week/month/year/life, Courtney Milan's The Marquis Who Mustn't is not only a deeply lovely book, but it can (speaking from experience) push back against near-overwhelming anxiety in a truly remarkable way with its deep faith in people's capacity for good. Highly rec!

Book #15 of 2024: JESUS’ SON by Denis Johnson, a book I reread once a year. “All these weirdos, and me getting a little better every day right in the midst of them. I had never known, never even imagined for a heartbeat, that there might be a place for people like us.”

Delighted to be celebrating publication day for my second BFI Film Classic. I’ve since lost 20kg and my chest measurement has come down 28cm, and my waist 8cm. 
My physical and mental health has improved mightily. If you can find the motivation, do it! 
You won’t be sorry.

Attended my first ever yoga class today. I felt affirmed, centered, grateful, and even powerful at some moments. It was so good. Afterward, I was in such a great mood. It’s rare for me to get that “endorphin rush” after exercise but I definitely felt it. Gonna be sore tomorrow but I’ll be back!

I think I've posted this before, but apparently I'm going through old ultimate frisbee photos. It turns out playing or practicing sports almost every day of the week because that's the only time you're comfortable in your body will make you kinda swole

I’m down more than 30 pounds now: no sugar, no red meat, no processed food, minimal dairy. I feel better than I have in years. I’d like to lose 10 more.

I weight lift because it's fun, great for women's health and helps tremendously with my C-PTSD.

Body composition aside, my vo2 and recovery rate are increasing. That’s more important, and I’m glad for it. Keep on running Dax.

The elevator in my office building says the weight limit is 3500 lbs OR 21 persons and I’m just wondering how many times 21 adult Americans would be at or below 3500 pounds.

I’d say it was a successful first in my weight loss journey. I was fasting for most of the day. I did a solid hour on he stairmaster and dinner was a fairly small, but high protein salad. It’s a bit daunting, but this isn’t the first time I’ve had to drop a lot of weight.

🌱 In #syracuse #ny this forsythia bush is blooming in my neighbor’s yard. It should bloom in the spring, so this is wicked early.   Flowers blooming out of season is a bad omen. For example, spring flowers blooming in the winter means the temperatures are too warm & that has lots of ramifications.

I've said this before, but it bears repeating: one of the most subtly horrible byproducts of "The Reagan Revolution" was the systematic transformation of quality basic and higher education into a luxury good.

a lot of academics work very hard to find the perfect, best, optimal answer, and this sits uneasily with the demands of groupwork and administration, which are frequently about expedience and satisficing
I think college prestige is net bad and that extension schools are very good, but I know a dude who went to Harvard Extension and now wears Harvard apparel every day—my man, you gotta chill out

Trying to solve for equilibrium: will red states ripping their own higher ed to shreds lead to better or worse outcomes for profs in blue states? Better: maybe some students will leave and enrollment will increase in blue states. Worse: negative wage premia to teach in highly desirable blue states
The glacial pace of academia means it takes ten years to be recognised for anything you’ve done (if you’re lucky) by which time you’re no longer doing it.

I increasingly feel my education policy views make me sound crotchety (schools should teach more rigorous math; teachers should have higher grading standards; test scores have a role to play in college admissions; college tends to pay off).

But I'd like to think it's evidence-based crotchetiness.

i saw these two articles recommended me and i feel like this is an issue not often talked about. community college frequently fails to prepare students for a bachelor's degree, exacerbated by its reputation as being "lesser" than universities, so it's good that california is trying to bridge the gap

We didn’t lose 100k academic jobs; there are still 100k ppl teaching classes.  

What changed is not the quantity of jobs but the quality.
Two of the most brilliant organizers of the WVU student protests are now running for the WV House of Delegates. Talk about universities providing an all-round education!

one of the dominant ideologies among this country's elite is College Admissions Brain where you reorient your entire worldview around valorizing the effective strategies for getting into good colleges as like, a really elaborate game whose winners get the good careers and losers don't.
Not to say any particular university closing isn't tragic, but an underappreciated indicator of shut-down-ness is whether you have less than 5 percent name recognition outside of your home county

My favorite part of academia is spending a lot of time developing comprehensive and detailed grant proposals that demonstrate I'm doing groundbreaking work but also the grant cannot pay me a stipend of more than thirty thousand dollars

Reminder that Harvard could admit twice as many students and provide all the resources to educate and support them without breaking a sweat. It doesn’t do this not because it can’t but because it doesn’t want to.

I am happy in my new job, but there are things I miss about academia. I miss the flexibility. I miss being a free agent. I miss having time to run whatever study I want. I miss mentoring. 

I think a lot of people have ambivalent feelings about leaving the academy, but I rarely see them on display.

It's a shame doctoral education is structured to be so exploitative and inaccessible for people who don't come from wealth/financial stability, because otherwise would be such a rewarding growth experience as you develop craft and watch yourself become an expert in something.

I'm once again stunned by the polite cruelty of academia, where brilliant young people are consistently chewed up, through no fault of their own. 

We who've achieved tenure and have stable jobs: we were smart, and hard working, and diligent. But we were also, to a ludicrous degree, stupidly lucky.

One of the most infuriating things about academia is how unbelievably SLOW everything is. Slow to success, slow to failure, slow to pay, slow to reject… the only recipe for success is unwavering patience & privilege to withstand soul crushing uncertainty

News of WVU’s “Academic Transformation” has died down.

43% (9/21) of the faculty in my unit (Div. of Plant & Soil Sciences) are being fired & we will learn our fate by next Friday (10/13).

The idea of losing ≈50% of my colleagues / friends (or 100% if I’m RIFed) by next summer is devastating!

West Virginia University not only getting rid of all its foreign language courses but also STEM fields like the math graduate program makes me just wonder what any of these private equity conservatives even want a university to be, theoretically. I assume just totally abolished?

As someone who is once again having to negotiate stupid rules about what door I can use & where I can sit for breakfast I remain convinced that academics at oxbridge shouldn’t be leading national research organisations b/c they are accultured to freaking weird hierarchical working environments

My experience after a brief stint as university management  I quit due to disgust) is that a lot of things that will improve the working conditions of students are completely possible, but never a priority because management refuse to humanize students and prefer to see them as numbers and money.

One of my Masters degrees is from City College. I had great teachers, an excellent education, and did not have to go into any debt. This was in the early 90s. The CUNY system along with the NYPL are the two best institutions that this city has produced. The politicians are gutting both.

Totally agree with the point here: While so much of the public discussion is about this or that imagined fault with elite universities, the land-grant schools are way more important to the country, and many of them are in financial crisis.

4/ Here's MIT's staff breakdown (wh. took a 3 second search):
Total staff: 16,327. Faculty: 1080. Sounds damning, right. Not Brooks' 8:1 ratio but 16:1.

Wait for it.

Of the 15,247 non-faculty staff, 4K are research staff--we do a lot of science and tech, and many of those who do aren't teachers...

The Ivy League discourse reminds me of a convo with Tom Cotton we had as law students. He complained to me that Harvard was full of libs, socialists, Jews and women. 
I grew up in north Texas. I went to Harvard Law because I was a striver. I didn’t want to live in Texas forever.
Maybe if "billionaire megadonors" weren't part of the educational ecosystem at all, universities wouldn't feel beholden to them.

“Why don’t you move to a blue state that likes education more?”

(Eric Adams: turns CUNY into a police overtime bank)

just as a point of fact: it is, of course, partially true that a news organization’s internal culture shapes what it covers and deems important — but i cannot understate how much *sincere reader interest* there is in elite college stories such as the harvard president’s resignation.

For faculty and trainees from historically excluded groups, diversity statements make traditionally invisible labor and expertise visible. In the absence of institutional support, these efforts will go back to being invisible and unvalued

Terrible news that the University of Kent is planning on cutting linguistics along with a range of other Humanities and Social Science courses. The kind of courses that give students the skills employers are looking for, but also the courses that help us make sense of the world.

I would argue that advanced degrees take too long in all disciplines. Back in the day, it was three years for a STEM Ph.D., and a little longer for the humanities.

I saw this on Twitter and I fucking love black hole science so I wanted to explain it -

A black hole with an event horizon of what looks to be about 1 foot across would contain the mass of more than 17 Earths and would have a gravitational pull around 30 Quadrillion times stronger than Earth’s.

Also. Earth is moving hella fast. We orbit the sun at 67,100 mph and orbit the center of the Milky Way galaxy at about 447,000 mph. This is while the Earth rotates on its axis at about 1,000 mph. Here’s a short clip about it and they guy has a funny accent.

The accretion disk for this black hole is SEVEN LIGHT YEARS across!

it’s a favourite.

A chuck and mushroom stew (button, cremini, shiitake, oyster, king oyster) over brown and wild rice.

My garden adventures were kind of a disaster thanks to the horrific unending rainfall of the summer, but I did manage to grow potatoes!  They’re beautiful!  I’m so proud.  I also grew some really pathetic daikon radishes

Siberian dwarf kale from expired seeds. Leggy but alive. I have been taking them outside in the gloomy winter sun a few hours a day in addition to using a grow light but they must need something more 🤔 🌱

A known anti-fungal in the garden, Marigold assists with human ailments, too! It’s very good for skin issues and discolorations. It promotes the growth of healthy cell tissue of all types. Digestive and menstrual issues can be assisted with Marigold teas or culinary infusions!
Gotta love when you can make more plants for free. Rooting the runners from CA Native strawberries. Smaller than normal but amazing flavor and a great ground cover for shady areas. 🌱
The sunchokes are blooming! For reference, the shortest yellow flowers are my height (5.5 ft). We figure the tallest stalks are about 11 or 13 feet. 🌱

I planted three lots of beans. Dwarf beans from old seeds that I wasn’t sure would sprout, and runner beans & dwarf beans that @@164020 gave to me. The old beans I had don’t seem to be sprouting, so far at least but they are doing a good job of growing fungi. 🌱 🇳🇿

🌱
This onion plant has bloomed a few times this year. The seeds make tons of babies and the bloom stalk is about 3 ft tall with such delicate lil flowers. Pots full of babies! 🌱🪴💚
my nasturtiums are so much happier this year than last. i didn’t even know they could climb, and look how high they got up the clematis trellis 🌱
I let this cucumber get huge to harvest its seeds to grow next year. It did so well. Almost 20 cucumbers from one plant!

#NowWatching Poor Things (2023) - A wonderful Frankenstein picaresque. Lanthimos continues to be one of the most interesting filmmakers working. I’m a little too tired and scattered this morning to really dig in, but following are a few (spoiler free) things that made me extremely happy: #filmsky /1

THE LOST BOYS is my favorite vampire movie ever. It’s got an incredible soundtrack, the two Coreys, and some guy named Kiefer. I love Technicolor films. Say what you want about Shumacher and his wild aesthetics. He sure nailed this one. Sometimes it’s better to stay off the boardwalk.🩸

Finished watching Rollerball. Brilliant. 

I especially like the fragments of subplot intentionally left only partly formed. It's there to put it together but it doesn't shove it down your throat.

Obviously a huge influence on Blood Bowl but also perhaps on 2000AD's Song of the Surfer

I loved DREAM SCENARIO (expanding wide Nov 22nd). The type of movie that takes a ridiculous, outlandish premise and explores its implications as fully as possible. It never loses sight of the human story at the center of the storm. And it delivers an amazing Late Stage Cage performance.

I never get tired of watching EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE. It's heartwarming, funny, thought-provoking, emotional, mind-blowing, and weird. It's genre-bending, the cinematography is brilliant, and the performances are phenomenal. It's a magnificent movie and Stephanie Hsu deserved that Oscar.📽️

Just got back from seeing End of Evangelion in theaters. It's one of my fav movies, and I've seen it lots of times on video, but never on the big screen until now.

It's such a unique film that will never be repeated. It's grim, cool, visceral, stylish and unapologetically disarming. A masterpiece.

FROM DUSK TILL DAWN was released 28 years ago this week. A collaboration between filmmaking pals Robert Rodriguez and Quentin Tarantino, and a cult vampire classic, the tale of how it came to be will have you off to visit the Titty Twister…

1/40

I feel like I’m the only person who likes Abel Ferrara’s Body Snatchers (1993). For me Don Siegel, Philip Kaufman and Ferrara all made really good movies. I’ve never seen the 2000s version but three out of four interesting movies is a very good track record.

Just rewatched BIG NIGHT. Fantastic, even though made when a film could be about a genuinely “big night” even though Galactus isn’t trying to eat the world and the story is just an exploration of brotherhood, with a lot of great Italian cooking thrown in as “special effects”.

As the foothold of the Normans, Wexford is a good place to discover medieval sites. Here's a few favourites:

Seafood Doria is a type of gratin that combines seafood, white sauce, cheese. It is made by layering rice, seafood, and sauce in a baking dish, and then baking it until the cheese is melted and bubbly. Seafood Doria is a popular dish in Japan, and is often served hot and enjoyed as a comfort food.

#GardeningFeed
Just harvested my first saffron of the year. 🌱 With the unusually pleasant warm October we've been having, these earliest flowers snuck up on me.

Tomatoes are finally just going gangbusters in my Aerogarden. I should have been pruning more aggressively, but this is my first try growing a batch of them, and I'm pretty jazzed about my results, regardless. 🌱

I wasn’t sure if these Caladiums would survive along the walkway to our front door. This area stays pretty damp and part of it gets a lot sun. The plants getting the most sun are definitely smaller than these gorgeous giants closest to the door. 😍😍😍 Zone 10b.
My spotted bee balm is blooming for the first time! I moved it into a sunnier spot a couple weeks ago and I’d say the move was successful. Please ignore the crispy epimedium around the edges. 🌱
🌱A small lemon bush I grew from seed of my mother in laws free fruiting lemon tree. It reverted to root stock, so I leave this one to butterflies. They love to lay their eggs here. 🥰

🌱 The Iris 'Katharine Hodgkin' has started flowering.
They even withstood the heavy rain of the last few days.

We have a ridiculous volume of California bluebell seeds and the temptation to seed-bomb with them is pretty high. Who wouldn’t love them? Good foliage, they die back completely to make room for summer blooms, and that BLUE—it’s unmatched 🌱

soo happy whenever this gets a bloom. I love the colors. its from a rose I have migrated from My late grandmother's garden. the plant itself is over 30 y.o. (she had it forever too). I have abused it (drought, heat, poor soil) but it persisits. 🌱🌹

put two rose bushes in this year from plug plants and expected little other than hoping they’d establish well for next year, and they keep surprising me! one has bloomed twice, and even the shyer climbing rose has decided to give me a little spray of new buds in september 🌱

this rose usually gets absolutely devastated by aphids but this year is doing well.  

i always give it a good prune but i cut it right back this autumn. is that what helped? more birds hanging around it too?

since i might lose all my blooms to tomorrow’s power washing disaster, i cut some for the first time. they’re too small & too few to do much with, but i grew them from seed and they’re very cheerful 🌱

I say again, Ivy League schools do not exist for the education, they exist for the connections. Most students go there to social climb.
if the harvards and yales of the world had any interest in benefitting society rather than just reproducing an elite class they would admit 10x as many undergrads

My favorite part about academia is how you get to pay taxes on reimbursements, which are reimbursing money you already paid taxes on. 
There really must be a better way...
Not all scholarship has to be paradigm-shifting, groundbreaking stuff. Sometimes, shit just needs to get done but we lack the time to actually DO it.

Leaving academia was hard. Being an academic was arguably the most important part of my identity for almost a decade. But it wasn’t right for me anymore.

Leaving has taught me that who I am is not job-dependent and that I have the power to create a better life for myself when things aren’t working.

David leonhardt gushing with wonder about why Defense Department k-8 schools, which are showered with funding and exclusively teach a student body that is adequately fed and housed, outperform state public school systems. Don’t worry, some Harvard people are on the case.

huh. that’s weird. the article itself ends up implying that, while things are absolutely better, market forces alone are not remedy for problems that are intrinsically sociopolitical in nature, such as biased employment environments and inherent access to opportunity. what is a term for this? DEI?

One thing that changed when I became a professor and which I don’t like is people paying attention to what I say because of my title rather than because I have insightful perspectives and demonstrate in-depth knowledge.

This is my leaking ceiling at CUNY Brooklyn College. Facility Operations tells me they have no ETA on repairs because they are triaging due to too many other problems they have to fix on campus due to years of deferred infrastructure spending.

Had a former student visit yesterday. Have to reflect on the fact that about half the people who have left my lab having been successful wound up in toxic situations in their subsequent labs. So many academics are awful.

Introducing Sample's Rule of 90 as it pertains to work in higher ed:

At any scale (committee, department, division, college), 90% of the time 90% of the work is done by 10% of the people.

The most horrifying thing about WVU is how hard faculty, staff, and students fought to save the integrity of their university, and how little it mattered to the board of governors. We are powerless to save higher education.

in america, there are two separate but equally idiotic groups: those who think they’re important because they went to an ivy league school, and those who believe people who went to ivy league schools are important. these are their stories.

One thing I’ve come to find annoying in academia—by brute force repetition—are people who deliberately tell you what a prestigious university they come from. Bonus if they explicitly use the word “Prestigious.”

Academic hack: Want to learn how to say no? Make a deal with a colleague: Every time you say no, they owe you a beer 🍺 (or other item of choice), and vice versa. We rarely cash in on this but it works -- nobody wants to be a loser and everybody wants all the beer. #academicsky

Higher education needs to be free. Cultivation of the mind is one of the highest purposes to which a human being can turn themselves, and there are millions of blue collar mines that lack only the time and tutelage to become geniuses.

Most free speech controversies are just a demand for censorship in disguise. The "solution" to college students yelling at a speaker, after all, is to force them to stop yelling.

The North Atlantic sea surface temperature is now up to an unbelievable 4.34 standard deviations above the recent 1991-2020 baseline climatology. Relative to that, the current anomaly is a 1-in-142,000-year event!

PSA: Just because a country or corporation did something “historic” to slow climate change, does not mean they did something laudable, effective, or in line with their responsibility.

It merely means they did more than they ever did—which, in most cases, is very little.

When you hear oil companies talking about “lower-carbon energy” they don’t actually mean renewables — it’s just code for “cleaner” fossil fuels, and it’s not even viable at scale. 

Their goal is to keep producing oil no matter what.

Perpetual Sunrise on Proxima Centauri b

Proxima Centauri b is an exoplanet orbiting the star nearest to our Sun. It was discovered in 2016 using the radial velocity method. The planet is likely tidally-locked to its parent star. Image from SpaceEngine.

We found the most distant (to-date) active black hole at the center of a galaxy ~570 Myrs after the Big Bang (>13.2B yrs ago)!! 🧪

Now with an official press release and fancy graphics from the fine people at Space Telescope Science Institute and NASA 😍

Apollo 11 launched OTD in 1969, carrying Neil Armstrong, Michael Collins, and Buzz Aldrin to the Moon. 

It was one of the most remarkable scientific collaborations in history, taking humans from Earth to the surface of another world, then returning them safely home.

Images: NASA 🧪

“supermassive black holes at the centers of galaxies feed on normal matter, allowing the black holes to generate magnetic fields that fire matter back out into the void. These winds collide with intergalactic material, which, in turn, affects the growth of new galaxies.” 🔭
It is exactly 3 days until Comet Kushida appears at its closest to Aldebaran (Alpha Tauri). Telescope required for visual observation.  🔭 🧪

An “interstellar shout” or perhaps a good ol’ Aussie “coo-ee” to #Voyager2 😁

Either way, an awesome job by the Voyager team NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory and our very own CSIRO crew here at the  Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex , restoring full contact with the Voyager 2 spacecraft.

Over the last few years, the Event Horizon Telescope has imaged the shadows of supermassive black holes at the centers of both M87 and our own Milky Way. 🧪 🔭 📡 ⚫️

Images: EHT Collaboration.

WOOOOOOT! First light with XRISM 🔭🛰️! The X-ray space telescope was launched in September, and carries an X-ray imager (Xtend) developed by JAXA and a spectrometer (Resolve) developed by NASA. There's images from both instruments in the JAXA press release ⏬

Ancient Olympic Games, quadrennial athletic event that was held in Olympia, Greece, from 776 BCE to about 393 CE. It was part of a religious festival that honoured Zeus, and the name Olympics was derived from Mount Olympus, home of the Greek gods.

Archaeologists in Germany have unearthed a sword from a Bronze Age burial, and the weapon is in such good condition that it still gleams. The 3,000-year-old sword, discovered in the town of Nördlingen in Bavaria, was found in the burial of a man, woman and child.

Universities often come really to matter to the people who study there. So much so that they come to understand the values that make universities communities what they are and to promote such values. If your community doesn’t foster such allegiance you might ask why.

Remember, academic success is not solely measured by research outputs or publications. It's also about the wellbeing and sense of belonging experienced by individuals within the academic community. Let's prioritize the whole person, not just their academic achievements.
If you're more concerned with the policies at Ivy League and other elite universities than the affordability of four year state schools, you're part of the problem.

A 🔥 article about sexism in higher education & a persuasive case for govts to

1. acknowledging that self-regulation isn’t working.
2. developing effective & transparent systems for measuring (in)equity in academia.
3. making funding in HE & research dependent on meeting of gender equity targets.

👇🏾

There are plenty of campus incidents that present difficult calls at the "academic freedom"/"discrimination" line. This (as reported) is not one of them. Holy moly.

The subhead doesn't do it justice either--this isn't just about verbal abuse. Read the whole thing.

tbh it makes a lot of sense that modern universities evolved in large part from monasteries, sexist institutions that produced and spread knowledge while primarily existing to legitimatize the power of elite failsons
I have taught at two elite universities and two small liberal arts colleges -- the students at WVU are the best students I've ever had anywhere. They deserve the whole world.

“Unfortunately, some people can’t seem to tell the difference between the kind of thinking, research & grounded argumentation that is the real work of universities & being “edgy” by spouting off poorly-argued, sophomorically-written & scientifically-unsound views that were “debated” a 100 years ago”

1 doctorate: smart enough, persistent, maybe rich

2 doctorates: maybe related fields? Clearly smart and persistent and very likely have some resources and/or very odd preferences

2+ doctorates: this person cannot prioritize and is a danger to themselves but nobody else

A lot of handwringing about the decline of the humanities. As @@82414 has shown, the reason we are suffering academically is lack of interest by students. Xphilosopher posted this picture and it's crystal clear: interest cratered since 2010.

Who needs a professorship when you're professorcore

Sometimes when you think you're tired of the system what you actually need is just a change of environment. At the end of my PhD I was pretty jaded and wasn't sure academia was for me any more. But my postdoc has renewed my optimism in a way I wouldn't have thought was possible!

God willing, I will never be a president of an elite university. And I know exactly what it is like to be the first Black woman hired somewhere and be under the most ridiculous scrutiny led by people who want to see you fail, not succeed and want you gone. 
apnews.com/article/harv...
Don’t whine to me about DEI. I spent 40 years in journalism watching mediocre white guys in management and corporate as we wrote about mediocre white guys in public office.

“There is a cost for success in STEM academia. The few minoritized people who seem to thrive might be doing what psychologists call ‘high-effort coping’”

True words by Jean King, and gosh, do I not feel it.

Can we do a connection of what happened at the Grace Hopper conférence to how most of the DEI positions went to white women

And how the failure to talk about that has hampered all real progress in real moderation and resource building for gender representation

okay then don't buy into the lie that DEI is responsible for putting unqualified people into different jobs. That's not what it does. That has never been what it does. The people telling you otherwise want a racial hierarchy enforced by the state.

“Prominent Harvard Academic” with the ear of the board.

昨晚和老贝开了一个多小时去Malibu 看流星雨，我没有任何支架，出门前照着小红书调了下手机相机。我把手机放到车顶，贴到前车窗，侧车窗，支到我的脚旁边拍的。

星河灿烂，流星确实看到了，但我其实并没有捕捉下，因为我没开到连拍，但其实即使连拍了也不知道啥时候有，基本上5分钟一颗。

图1，不是流星是灰机千万别被我哄骗。图3中间那颗最亮的最大的，我边界没拍清晰的是Jupiter 土星，太阳系最大的行星。

3点左右往回开，实话说我是有点担心的，全是弯道小黑路，脖子疼，瞌睡，一到家就躺倒了。

虽然拍照木有捕捉到流星，但生平第一次看到了流星，许了愿，下一步希望愿成，足矣～

보통 "대놓고 성적인" 게임은 게이머에게는 적신호인데요. 뭐, 아주 공정하게 말하자면 게임이 어떤 미감aesthetics을 세일즈 포인트로 잡는 것 자체는 많은 게임들에게서 발견되는 편입니다. 다만 "대놓고 성적인" 경우에, 확실히 말할 수 있는 건, 대중성을 완전히 내던졌다는 겁니다.

닌텐도가 가족용 게임에 집중하는 이유도, 대중성 획득에 따른 수익의 극대화입니다. 이건 본래 콘솔 게임기가 거실의 TV를 통해 출력되고 어쩌구 있지만 일단 생략하고요. 디즈니와 마찬가지로 닌텐도 역시 부모를 고객으로 보고 있다 생각하시면 됩니다.
